name: Adenosine
indications: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (SVT / AVNRT / AVRT) — first-line pharmacological treatment after failed vagal manoeuvres
Diagnosis of broad-complex tachycardia of uncertain origin
contraindications: Second or third degree AV block (unless pacemaker in situ)
Sick sinus syndrome (unless pacemaker in situ)
Asthma or severe COPD (may provoke severe bronchospasm)
Atrial flutter or fibrillation with accessory pathway (Wolff-Parkinson-White) — risk of VF
Irregular broad-complex tachycardia (may be pre-excited AF)
precautions: Warn patient of transient chest tightness, flushing, and sense of impending doom — normal and brief
Must be administered as rapid IV bolus followed immediately by 20 mL saline flush
Have defibrillator and resuscitation equipment immediately available
Effect potentiated by dipyridamole — reduce dose to 3 mg
Effect antagonised by theophyllines — higher doses may be required
Cardiac monitoring (ECG) mandatory throughout
sideEffects: Transient flushing, chest tightness, dyspnoea (seconds only)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Slows conduction through the AV node via A1 adenosine receptor activation, causing transient AV block. Interrupts re-entrant circuits dependent on AV nodal conduction (AVNRT, AVRT), restoring sinus rhythm.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extremely rapid — taken up and metabolised by erythrocytes and endothelial cells within seconds. Converted to inosine and AMP.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Plasma half-life <10 seconds. Not renally excreted in active form.

adultDosing.notes: Administer as fast IV bolus into antecubital or central vein. Immediately follow with 20 mL 0.9% NaCl flush pushed rapidly. Patient will experience 5–15 seconds of chest discomfort/flushing — warn beforehand. Monitor ECG continuously.

adultDosing.indication: SVT — second dose (if first unsuccessful after 1–2 min)
adultDosing.notes: If 6 mg is ineffective after 1–2 minutes, give 12 mg rapid IV bolus with 20 mL flush. May repeat 12 mg once more if still unsuccessful.

paediatricDosing.frequency: May double dose and repeat every 1–2 minutes
paediatricDosing.maxDose: 0.3 mg/kg or 12 mg per dose (whichever is lower)
paediatricDosing.notes: Give as rapid IV bolus with immediate 5–10 mL saline flush. Initial 0.1 mg/kg; if unsuccessful after 1–2 min, increase to 0.2 mg/kg; if still unsuccessful, 0.3 mg/kg (max 12 mg). Neonates: 0.05 mg/kg initial dose.

name: Adrenaline (Epinephrine)
indications: Cardiac arrest (VF, pulseless VT, PEA, asystole)
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in cardiac arrest or life-threatening anaphylaxis
precautions: Cardiac arrest: administer IV/IO after second defibrillation for shockable rhythms
Anaphylaxis: IM preferred over IV — IV carries risk of severe hypertension and dysrhythmia
Caution in patients with ischaemic heart disease (non-arrest use)
Extravasation causes tissue necrosis — use large vein or IO
High-dose adrenaline not recommended in standard cardiac arrest

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Directly stimulates α1, α2, β1, and β2 adrenoreceptors. α1 activation causes vasoconstriction increasing SVR and diastolic BP; β1 activation increases heart rate and contractility; β2 activation causes bronchodilation and vasodilation in skeletal muscle.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Rapidly metabolised by monoamine oxidase (MAO) and catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) in the liver, kidneys, and plasma to inactive metabolites (metanephrine, VMA).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Excreted renally as inactive conjugated metabolites. Plasma half-life is 2–3 minutes.

adultDosing.maxDose: No defined maximum during active resuscitation
adultDosing.notes: Administer after second defibrillation for shockable rhythms (VF/pVT). For non-shockable rhythms (PEA/asystole), give as soon as IV/IO access obtained. Flush with 20mL normal saline.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat every 5–15 minutes if no improvement
adultDosing.notes: Inject into outer mid-thigh (vastus lateralis). IM route preferred in anaphylaxis. 1:1,000 concentration for IM use.

adultDosing.maxDose: Titrate to effect under medical direction
adultDosing.notes: IV adrenaline reserved for cardiovascular collapse unresponsive to IM dosing. Dilute and administer slowly. Risk of severe hypertension and dysrhythmia. Consider infusion if persistent.

paediatricDosing.notes: Administer 0.01mg/kg IV/IO. Use 1:10,000 (0.1mg/mL) concentration. Flush with 3–5mL normal saline.
Use 1:1,000 concentration for IM. Inject into outer mid-thigh. EpiPen Jr (0.15mg) for 15–30kg, EpiPen (0.3mg) for >30kg as alternative if ampoule unavailable.

name: Adrenaline Auto-Injector (EpiPen)
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in life-threatening anaphylaxis
precautions: Inject into outer mid-thigh (through clothing if necessary)
Avoid buttocks injection (risk of sciatic nerve injury)
Caution in patients with known cardiac disease — benefits outweigh risk in anaphylaxis
Monitor for rebound anaphylaxis

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Same pharmacodynamic profile as adrenaline (epinephrine): stimulates α and β adrenoreceptors to reverse anaphylaxis via vasoconstriction, increased cardiac output, bronchodilation, and inhibition of mast cell mediator release.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Rapidly metabolised by MAO and COMT in the liver and peripheral tissues to inactive metabolites.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites. Half-life approximately 2–3 minutes; IM absorption from thigh achieves peak plasma levels in 8–10 minutes.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat after 5–15 minutes if no improvement
adultDosing.notes: Inject into outer mid-thigh. Hold device in place for 3–10 seconds. Seek further care immediately.

paediatricDosing.frequency: May repeat after 5–15 minutes if no improvement
paediatricDosing.notes: EpiPen Jr (0.15mg) for children 15–30kg. Use EpiPen (0.3mg) for children >30kg. Inject into outer mid-thigh.

name: Amiodarone
indications: Pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT) refractory to defibrillation
Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) refractory to other treatment
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to amiodarone or iodine
Second- or third-degree AV block without pacemaker
precautions: Contains benzyl alcohol and polysorbate 80 — avoid in neonates
Hypotension common with rapid IV administration — dilute and slow infusion
Prolongs QT interval — monitor ECG
Phlebitis with peripheral IV — use large vein or central line when possible
Incompatible with many drugs — flush line before and after
Thyroid function monitoring required long-term (not relevant in acute prehospital use)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Predominantly a Class III antiarrhythmic (potassium channel blocker) that prolongs the cardiac action potential and refractory period. Also has Class I (sodium channel), Class II (non-competitive β-blockade), and Class IV (calcium channel) properties. Effective for both supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensively metabolised in the liver, primarily via CYP3A4 and CYP2C8, to the active metabolite desethylamiodarone. Hepatic first-pass is minimal with IV dosing.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Primarily biliary/faecal excretion. Minimal renal excretion. Extremely long half-life: 40–55 days. Drug and metabolite persist in tissues (particularly fat and lung) for months.

adultDosing.frequency: Initial bolus; may give further 150mg after 5th shock
adultDosing.notes: Administer 300mg IV/IO bolus after 3rd defibrillation attempt. Dilute in 20mL 5% dextrose. Further 150mg may be given if VF/pVT persists. Flush with normal saline.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat 150mg bolus for recurrent VT; then infusion under medical direction
adultDosing.maxDose: 300mg for repeat boluses; ongoing infusion under medical direction
adultDosing.notes: Dilute in 100mL 5% dextrose. Administer over 10 minutes. Monitor BP and ECG continuously. Use largest available peripheral vein.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Single dose after 3rd defibrillation; may repeat once
paediatricDosing.notes: 5mg/kg IV/IO bolus after 3rd defibrillation attempt. May repeat 5mg/kg after 5th shock. Dilute in 5% dextrose.

name: Aspirin
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to aspirin or NSAIDs
Active GI bleeding or peptic ulcer disease
Children <16 years (Reye's syndrome risk) — except Kawasaki disease under specialist direction
precautions: Aspirin-sensitive asthma (may precipitate bronchospasm)
Renal impairment
Concurrent anticoagulant therapy — increased bleeding risk
Third trimester of pregnancy
Elderly — increased risk of GI bleeding

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) and COX-2 enzymes by acetylation, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis in platelets (antiplatelet effect lasting platelet lifespan ~7–10 days) and reducing prostaglandin synthesis (anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antipyretic at higher doses).
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Rapidly hydrolysed to salicylic acid by plasma and hepatic esterases. Salicylate undergoes hepatic conjugation (glycine and glucuronic acid).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of salicylate and conjugates; pH-dependent. Aspirin half-life ~15–20 minutes; salicylate half-life 2–3 hours (analgesic doses) up to 15–30 hours (anti-inflammatory doses).

adultDosing.notes: Chew tablet for faster buccal absorption — do not swallow whole. Administer as early as possible once ACS suspected. Use dispersible tablet if available.
Take with food or milk to reduce GI irritation. Not recommended as routine EMS analgesia when opioids or other analgesics available.

paediatricDosing.notes: Aspirin is contraindicated in children <16 years due to risk of Reye's syndrome. Exception: Kawasaki disease under specialist direction only.

name: Atropine and Pralidoxime Cl (DuoDote)
indications: Nerve agent (chemical warfare agent) poisoning — e.g. sarin, tabun, VX
contraindications: No absolute contraindications when life-threatening cholinergic poisoning present
Myasthenia gravis (atropine component may precipitate crisis — relative)
precautions: Administer only in confirmed or strongly suspected organophosphate/nerve agent exposure
Pralidoxime is most effective when given early — within minutes to hours of exposure; limited benefit after 24–48 hours
Monitor for atropine toxicity (flushing, tachycardia, urinary retention, hyperthermia)
Maintain airway — excessive secretions are primary cause of death
Pralidoxime is NOT effective for carbamate poisoning
Decontaminate patient before treatment when safe to do so
DuoDote auto-injector delivers 2.1mg atropine + 600mg pralidoxime Cl per injection
sideEffects: Atropine: tachycardia, dry mouth, urinary retention, confusion, hyperthermia
Pralidoxime: dizziness, diplopia, headache, nausea, tachycardia at rapid IV rates

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Atropine: competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, reversing cholinergic excess (bradycardia, bronchospasm, secretions, miosis). Pralidoxime (2-PAM): reactivates organophosphate-inhibited acetylcholinesterase, preventing permanent enzyme aging; most effective if given early.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Atropine: hepatic hydrolysis and conjugation. Pralidoxime: minimal hepatic metabolism.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Atropine: ~50% renal excretion unchanged; half-life 2–3 hours. Pralidoxime: primarily renal excretion unchanged; half-life ~1–2 hours.

adultDosing.indication: Mild organophosphate / nerve agent poisoning (miosis, rhinorrhoea, mild secretions)
adultDosing.frequency: Repeat every 10–15 minutes if symptoms not improving
adultDosing.maxDose: 3 auto-injectors for self/buddy-aid; additional doses under medical direction
adultDosing.notes: Inject into outer mid-thigh or buttock. Wait 10–15 minutes after first injection before administering second. Reassess SLUDGE symptoms (Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defaecation, GI distress, Emesis).

adultDosing.indication: Severe organophosphate / nerve agent poisoning (unconscious, seizures, respiratory distress, severe secretions)
adultDosing.frequency: Additional injections every 10–15 minutes under medical direction
adultDosing.maxDose: 3 initial injectors; further doses per medical protocol
adultDosing.notes: Administer 3 injectors immediately for severe/life-threatening symptoms. Prioritise airway management and oxygenation. Evacuate immediately.

paediatricDosing.dose: Use with caution; auto-injector not recommended for children <15kg or under specialist guidance
paediatricDosing.notes: DuoDote auto-injector is designed for adult use. In paediatric nerve agent exposure, use separate atropine (0.02mg/kg IV/IM) and pralidoxime (25–50mg/kg IV) under medical direction. Consult toxicology/medical direction.

name: Atropine Sulphate
indications: Symptomatic bradycardia (haemodynamically significant sinus bradycardia, AV block)
precautions: Small doses (<0.5mg) in adults may cause paradoxical bradycardia — minimum 0.5mg dose
Doses <0.1mg in children may cause paradoxical bradycardia — minimum 0.1mg
Caution in patients with myocardial ischaemia — tachycardia increases oxygen demand
Caution in febrile patients — may impair sweating and worsen hyperthermia
High-degree AV block (Mobitz II, complete heart block) — pacing preferred
May cause urinary retention in males with prostatic hypertrophy

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (M1–M3). Increases heart rate by blocking vagal tone on the SA node (chronotropic), reduces secretions, causes bronchodilation, and dilates pupils. Used in bradycardia, organophosphate toxicity, and pre-procedure.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Partially hydrolysed in the liver to tropine and tropic acid. Approximately 50% excreted unchanged.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Primarily renal excretion (unchanged drug and metabolites). Half-life 2–3 hours; shorter in children.

adultDosing.notes: Administer as IV bolus. Target HR >60 bpm with resolution of symptoms. If atropine ineffective, consider transcutaneous pacing or adrenaline infusion.

adultDosing.frequency: Repeat every 5–10 minutes until secretions dry (atropinisation achieved)
adultDosing.maxDose: No defined maximum — titrate to drying of secretions
adultDosing.notes: Endpoint is drying of secretions, not tachycardia. Very large doses may be required in severe poisoning (>20mg total). IM acceptable if IV access delayed.

paediatricDosing.notes: Minimum dose 0.1mg in children (smaller doses may cause paradoxical bradycardia). Maximum single dose 0.5mg. May give IO if IV not available.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Repeat every 5–10 minutes until secretions dry
paediatricDosing.maxDose: 2mg per dose; no defined total maximum in severe poisoning
paediatricDosing.notes: Titrate to drying of secretions. May require large cumulative doses in severe poisoning.

name: Benzatropine Mesylate
indications: Acute drug-induced dystonic reactions (e.g. from antipsychotics, metoclopramide, droperidol, prochlorperazine)
Acute extrapyramidal side effects including oculogyric crisis, torticollis, opisthotonos
precautions: Use with caution in the elderly — risk of confusion, urinary retention, constipation
Caution in hot weather — impairs sweating, risk of hyperthermia
May cause tachycardia — caution in tachyarrhythmias
Symptoms may recur 6–12 hours after administration as drug wears off — ensure hospital follow-up
Avoid in patients already taking significant anticholinergic medications

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (predominantly M1) in the CNS and periphery. Restores the dopamine/acetylcholine balance in the striatum disrupted by dopamine antagonists, reversing extrapyramidal symptoms.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism. Onset of action: IM 15–30 min; IV 1–5 min.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites. Half-life approximately 12–24 hours.

adultDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat once after 30 minutes if inadequate response
adultDosing.notes: IM route preferred in non-life-threatening dystonia. IV reserved for severe reactions (e.g. laryngospasm, oculogyric crisis causing airway risk). Symptoms typically resolve within 15–30 minutes IM or 1–5 minutes IV. Advise patient symptoms may recur — oral follow-up with prescriber required.

paediatricDosing.notes: Do not use in children <3 years. Use 0.02 mg/kg IM. Maximum single dose 2 mg. Diphenhydramine is an alternative if benzatropine unavailable.

name: Calcium Gluconate
indications: Hydrofluoric acid burns (topical gel or IV for systemic fluoride poisoning)
contraindications: Digoxin toxicity (calcium potentiates digoxin toxicity — avoid IV calcium)
precautions: Avoid rapid IV injection — risk of bradycardia, hypotension and cardiac arrest
Extravasation causes tissue necrosis — use large bore IV in a large vein
Incompatible with sodium bicarbonate and phosphates (precipitates)
Caution in patients on digoxin
In hyperkalaemia, calcium gluconate provides membrane stabilisation only — does not lower potassium level
10% calcium gluconate contains 0.225mmol/mL calcium (vs 10% calcium chloride which contains 0.68mmol/mL)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Provides exogenous calcium ions which stabilise cardiac myocyte membranes by restoring the resting membrane potential threshold (membrane stabilisation), antagonising the effects of hyperkalaemia and hypocalcaemia on cardiac conduction. Does not lower serum potassium.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Calcium gluconate is hydrolysed in plasma releasing free ionised calcium. Calcium is incorporated into bone, muscle, and other tissues as part of normal physiology.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Regulated primarily by kidneys (tubular reabsorption); excess calcium excreted in urine. Parathyroid hormone and calcitriol modulate renal handling.

adultDosing.frequency: Over 2–5 minutes; may repeat after 5 minutes if ECG changes persist
adultDosing.notes: ECG monitoring essential. Does not lower serum potassium — provides cardiac membrane stabilisation only. Effect onset 1–3 minutes, duration 30–60 minutes.

adultDosing.frequency: Over 5–10 minutes; repeat as required under medical direction
adultDosing.maxDose: Under medical direction — high-dose calcium therapy may be needed
adultDosing.notes: High-dose calcium for CCB toxicity may require far greater doses under specialist guidance (High Dose Insulin-Euglycaemia therapy may also be indicated). Monitor BP and ECG.
Administer slowly over 10 minutes. ECG monitoring recommended.

paediatricDosing.notes: ECG monitoring essential during administration. Administer slowly. May be given IO if IV unavailable.

name: Cefazolin
indications: Sepsis (empirical antibiotic therapy in remote/FIFO context)
Community-acquired pneumonia (mild–moderate, where IV access established)
contraindications: History of severe (anaphylactic) reaction to penicillins (cross-reactivity ~1–2%)
precautions: Cross-reactivity with penicillin allergy — use with caution; avoid if history of anaphylaxis to penicillin
Renal impairment — dose reduction required
Renal dialysis patients — dose adjustment
Pregnancy Category A — generally considered safe
History of GI disease, particularly colitis

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: First-generation cephalosporin. Bactericidal: binds to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), inhibiting bacterial cell wall (peptidoglycan) synthesis, leading to cell lysis. Active against gram-positive organisms (Staphylococcus, Streptococcus) and some gram-negatives.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Negligible hepatic metabolism; excreted almost entirely unchanged.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Primarily renal excretion by glomerular filtration and tubular secretion. Half-life 1.8–2 hours; prolonged significantly in renal impairment.

adultDosing.notes: Administer by slow IV injection over 3–5 minutes or IV infusion over 30 minutes. Reconstitute with sterile water or normal saline. First dose as early as possible in sepsis.

adultDosing.indication: Skin and soft tissue infection / wound infection
adultDosing.notes: IM injection painful — use large muscle. IV preferred. Duration determined by clinical response and evacuation timeline.

paediatricDosing.notes: Administer by slow IV injection over 3–5 minutes. IO route acceptable if IV unavailable. Neonates: 20mg/kg every 12 hours.

name: Ceftriaxone
indications: Suspected bacterial meningitis or meningococcal septicaemia (empirical, before lumbar puncture)
Severe skin and soft tissue infections
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to ceftriaxone or other cephalosporins
Known severe penicillin allergy with cross-reactivity (use with caution — cross-reactivity ~1–2%)
Neonates <41 weeks corrected gestational age or jaundiced — competes with bilirubin for albumin binding
Do not mix with calcium-containing IV solutions (risk of precipitation)
precautions: Cross-reactivity with penicillin allergy is low (~1–2%) but assess allergy history before administering
Do not administer simultaneously with calcium-containing fluids in the same IV line
Renal impairment: dose adjustment not usually required unless combined with hepatic impairment
Can cause biliary sludging with prolonged use — not an acute concern
Administer as slow IV injection over 2–4 minutes or IM
sideEffects: Local pain at IM injection site

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Bactericidal — inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), preventing peptidoglycan cross-linking. Active against a wide range of Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms including Neisseria meningitidis, Streptococcus pneumoniae, and most Enterobacteriaceae.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Not significantly metabolised. Protein-bound ~85–95%.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Excreted in bile (~40%) and urine (~60%). Half-life 6–9 hours, allowing once-daily dosing.

adultDosing.route: IV (slow injection over 2–4 min) or IM
adultDosing.notes: Do not delay administration for transport — give immediately on clinical suspicion. IM into gluteus or lateral thigh if IV access unavailable. IV is preferred in haemodynamically compromised patients.

adultDosing.route: IV (slow injection over 2–4 min) or IM
adultDosing.notes: Give 2 g for most serious infections. For community-acquired pneumonia 1–2 g is appropriate. Consult on-call doctor or follow local protocol for specific indications.

paediatricDosing.notes: Do not use in neonates with jaundice. Give 100 mg/kg (max 2 g) IV/IM immediately on clinical suspicion. Meningococcal rash + fever = treat immediately without waiting for transport.

name: Chloramphenicol Eye Drops
indications: Prophylaxis of infection following corneal abrasion or superficial foreign body removal
contraindications: Personal or family history of blood dyscrasias (aplastic anaemia associated with systemic chloramphenicol — rare with topical use but extreme caution advised)
Viral or fungal ocular infections (not effective)
precautions: Topical use only — do NOT use for systemic injection
Contact lens wearers: remove lenses before instilling drops; do not reinsert until infection resolved
Prolonged use may result in overgrowth of non-susceptible organisms
Risk of serious blood dyscrasias extremely rare with topical ophthalmic use but should be considered
Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding: consult on-call doctor
Requires on-call doctor authorisation prior to administration in the remote setting
sideEffects: Transient stinging or burning on instillation
Aplastic anaemia — extremely rare with topical use but documented

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit of susceptible bacteria, inhibiting peptidyl transferase activity and thereby blocking bacterial protein synthesis. Bacteriostatic; broad-spectrum activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms including Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella species.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Topical ophthalmic application results in minimal systemic absorption. Any absorbed drug undergoes hepatic conjugation to inactive glucuronide metabolites.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Minimal systemic absorption with topical use. Systemically absorbed drug is excreted renally (primarily as inactive glucuronide conjugate). Plasma half-life of systemic chloramphenicol is approximately 4 hours.

adultDosing.frequency: Every 2 hours while awake for first 48 hours, then every 4 hours for up to 5 days total
adultDosing.notes: Requires on-call doctor authorisation. Instil into lower conjunctival fornix. Advise patient to keep tip sterile and discard drops after treatment course.

adultDosing.indication: Post-foreign body removal / corneal abrasion prophylaxis
adultDosing.frequency: Every 4 hours while awake for 48–72 hours
adultDosing.notes: Requires on-call doctor authorisation. For prophylaxis only — reassess at 48 hours.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Every 2 hours while awake for first 48 hours, then every 4 hours for up to 5 days
paediatricDosing.notes: Requires on-call doctor authorisation. Not routinely recommended under 2 years without specialist advice. Technique as per adult dosing.

name: Cophenylcaine
indications: Topical anaesthesia for nasopharyngeal airway insertion
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to lignocaine (amide-type local anaesthetics) or phenylephrine
precautions: Do not exceed maximum lignocaine dose — particularly relevant in multiple applications
Phenylephrine component may cause transient hypertension and reflex bradycardia
Caution in patients with cardiovascular disease or severe hypertension
Caution in patients with hyperthyroidism
Avoid systemic absorption — topical use only
Lignocaine toxicity risk if large volumes applied to highly vascular nasal mucosa
sideEffects: Transient burning or stinging on application
Systemic lignocaine toxicity (rare with topical use): tingling, metallic taste, arrhythmia

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Combination of lignocaine (lignocaine) 5% and phenylephrine 0.5%. Lignocaine blocks voltage-gated sodium channels producing mucosal anaesthesia. Phenylephrine is a selective α1-adrenoreceptor agonist causing nasal vasoconstriction and decongestion.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Lignocaine: hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4/CYP1A2 to active metabolites (MEGX, GX). Phenylephrine: hepatic and intestinal MAO metabolism.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Lignocaine metabolites renally excreted; half-life 1.5–2 hours systemic, but topical absorption is limited. Phenylephrine excreted renally as sulphate conjugates.

adultDosing.frequency: Single application; allow 3–5 minutes for effect
adultDosing.maxDose: Do not exceed 4 sprays per nostril
adultDosing.notes: Spray into each nostril to be used. Allow 3–5 minutes for lignocaine anaesthesia and phenylephrine vasoconstriction to take effect before procedure. Use atomiser or spray device.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Single application; allow 3–5 minutes for effect
paediatricDosing.notes: Use with caution in small children. Phenylephrine may cause pronounced hypertension in young children. Dose conservatively.

name: Dexamethasone
indications: Severe allergic reactions and anaphylaxis (adjunct — after adrenaline)
Cerebral oedema (adjunct in head injury, brain tumour)
contraindications: Systemic fungal infection (relative — use only if benefit outweighs risk)
Live vaccines within 3 months (not relevant in emergency setting)
precautions: Single dose in emergency is safe — chronic use cautions do not apply to acute administration
Diabetes: monitor blood glucose — dexamethasone causes significant hyperglycaemia
Immunocompromised patients: may mask signs of infection
GI bleeding history: caution with repeated dosing
Psychiatric history: may precipitate acute psychosis with high doses

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Potent synthetic glucocorticoid (25× more potent than hydrocortisone). Binds glucocorticoid receptors, inhibiting phospholipase A2 and reducing arachidonic acid metabolites (prostaglandins, leukotrienes). Suppresses inflammatory cytokine production. Reduces vascular permeability and oedema formation.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism. Oral bioavailability ~80%. Onset of anti-inflammatory effect: 2–6 hours. Half-life 36–54 hours (biological effect much longer than plasma half-life).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion as metabolites. Minimal active renal excretion.

adultDosing.notes: Single dose is effective. Oral dexamethasone is as effective as IM for croup. Effect begins within 1–2 hours.

adultDosing.indication: Severe allergic reaction / anaphylaxis (adjunct after adrenaline)
adultDosing.notes: Adjunct only — adrenaline is first-line. Dexamethasone onset is delayed (2–6 hours) and does not replace adrenaline. May reduce late-phase biphasic reaction.
For vasogenic oedema (brain tumour, abscess). Not recommended for traumatic brain injury (no evidence of benefit, potential harm). Consult before administering in TBI.

paediatricDosing.notes: Oral route is as effective as IM and preferred (less distressing). Can use IV dexamethasone preparation given orally. Onset 1–2 hours. For moderate croup (Westley score 3–5) combine with nebulised adrenaline.
Alternative to prednisolone. Single dose of dexamethasone 0.3 mg/kg is equivalent to 3-day prednisolone course.

name: Dextrose 5% (D5W)
indications: Diluent for IV drug reconstitution and infusion (e.g. amiodarone, lignocaine infusions)
Mild hypoglycaemia where oral glucose cannot be given (if higher-concentration glucose unavailable)
contraindications: Head injury / raised intracranial pressure (hyperglycaemia worsens neurological outcomes — avoid as resuscitation fluid)
Ischaemic stroke (may worsen outcomes — avoid as resuscitation fluid)
Uncorrected hyperkalaemia (glucose-insulin therapy may be beneficial but dextrose alone is not first-line)
precautions: Not suitable as primary resuscitation fluid in haemorrhagic shock or hypovolaemia — use crystalloid (Hartmann's / Normal Saline)
5% dextrose is effectively free water once glucose is metabolised — will not expand intravascular volume
Avoid in hyperglycaemia
Monitor blood glucose levels during infusion
Rapid infusion of large volumes may cause hyponatraemia
sideEffects: Hyperglycaemia (with large volumes or in diabetic patients)
Phlebitis at IV site (less irritating than hypertonic solutions)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Provides free water and glucose. Glucose is a direct substrate for cellular energy (ATP) production via glycolysis and the Krebs cycle. D5W is isotonic in the bag but becomes effectively hypotonic once glucose is metabolised, distributing free water across all body fluid compartments.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Glucose is phosphorylated to glucose-6-phosphate and enters glycolysis, producing pyruvate → acetyl-CoA → Krebs cycle → ATP. Excess glucose stored as glycogen or converted to triglycerides.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: CO2 and water are the end products of aerobic glucose metabolism, exhaled and renally excreted respectively. Excess glucose spills into urine above the renal threshold (~10 mmol/L).

adultDosing.indication: Drug diluent / vehicle for IV infusions
adultDosing.notes: Commonly used to dilute amiodarone for infusion. Not preferred as resuscitation fluid. Check drug compatibility — many drugs incompatible with dextrose solutions.

paediatricDosing.notes: Use as directed per specific drug protocol. Not suitable as primary resuscitation fluid in paediatric patients. For hypoglycaemia in children, use 10% dextrose (glucose 10% IV).

name: Droperidol
indications: Acute agitation / behavioural emergency (chemical sedation adjunct)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to droperidol or butyrophenones
Known or suspected QT prolongation (QTc >450ms in males, >470ms in females)
precautions: QT prolongation — ECG monitoring recommended if available; avoid combining with other QT-prolonging agents
Extrapyramidal side effects (acute dystonia) — have benztropine available
Hypotension — particularly with rapid IV injection or in hypovolaemic patients
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (rare)
Sedation — monitor airway and respiratory status
Caution in elderly — increased sensitivity and risk of falls
May lower seizure threshold
sideEffects: QT prolongation and torsades de pointes (rare at low doses)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Butyrophenone antipsychotic and antiemetic. Primarily antagonises central dopamine D2 receptors (mesolimbic and chemoreceptor trigger zone), producing sedation, antiemesis, and antipsychotic effects. Also has α1-adrenergic blocking and weak anticholinergic properties.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensively metabolised in the liver, primarily by N-dealkylation and hydroxylation via CYP3A4.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites (75%) and faecal (22%). Half-life approximately 12 hours. QT prolongation risk; ECG monitoring recommended.

adultDosing.frequency: Stat; may repeat after 30–60 minutes if needed
adultDosing.maxDose: 5mg per episode under standard prehospital protocols
adultDosing.notes: Low-dose IV (0.625mg) is effective antiemetic with minimal side effects. For IM use, 2.5mg is standard dose. Onset IV 3–5 minutes; IM 15–20 minutes.

adultDosing.dose: 5mg IM (may combine with midazolam under protocol)
adultDosing.frequency: Stat; may repeat after 20 minutes under medical direction
adultDosing.notes: In FIFO/remote context, IM droperidol ± midazolam for acute severe agitation. Ensure airway monitoring. ECG when available. Document indication and vital signs.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Stat; may repeat once after 30–60 minutes
paediatricDosing.notes: Use with caution in children. Extrapyramidal effects more common in younger patients. Monitor airway and sedation.

name: Enoxaparin (Clexane)
indications: NSTEMI / unstable angina — anticoagulation as part of ACS management
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to enoxaparin, heparin, or pork products
Epidural/spinal anaesthesia in patients on therapeutic LMWH
precautions: Renal impairment: reduce dose in eGFR <30 — risk of accumulation and bleeding
Elderly (>75 years): reduce dose to 0.75 mg/kg SC for STEMI
Weigh against bleeding risk — particularly prior surgery, GI bleeding, or concurrent antiplatelet therapy
Administer SC — do not administer IM (risk of haematoma)
Anti-Xa monitoring not typically required for standard dosing
Once given, effect lasts 12 hours — inform receiving hospital

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Binds antithrombin III, potentiating its inhibition of factor Xa (primary mechanism) and thrombin (IIa). Prevents fibrin clot extension without dissolving existing thrombus. More predictable pharmacokinetics than unfractionated heparin; no need for routine monitoring.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Renal excretion, primarily unchanged. SC bioavailability ~100%.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal. Half-life ~4–5 hours SC. Anti-Xa activity detectable for up to 12 hours.

adultDosing.maxDose: No absolute maximum; 1 mg/kg per dose (avoid >100 mg per dose in obese patients without monitoring)
adultDosing.notes: Pre-hospital: single 1 mg/kg SC dose with aspirin and antiplatelet. Continue every 12 hours at hospital. For age ≥75 years with STEMI: 0.75 mg/kg (no loading dose). Inject into abdomen (avoid periumbilical area). Do not expel air bubble from syringe before injecting.

adultDosing.dose: 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily (or 1 mg/kg twice daily)
adultDosing.frequency: Once daily for outpatient PE/DVT; twice daily for high-risk
adultDosing.notes: For confirmed or highly suspected PE/DVT requiring anticoagulation. Single 1.5 mg/kg SC dose pre-hospital is appropriate before imaging confirmation. Inform hospital. Contraindicated if SBP <90 or suspected massive PE requiring thrombolysis.

paediatricDosing.notes: Seek specialist/medical direction before administering to children. Dose varies by age. Neonates: 1.5 mg/kg every 12 hours.

name: Fentanyl
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to fentanyl or opioids
Concurrent or recent MAO inhibitor use (within 14 days)
precautions: Respiratory depression — have naloxone available; monitor SpO₂ and respiratory rate
Hypotension — caution in hypovolaemic patients
Elderly or frail patients — reduce dose by 50%
Hepatic or renal impairment — reduced clearance
Head injury — use with caution; may mask neurological changes
Concurrent CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, alcohol) — additive respiratory depression
Chest wall rigidity with rapid high-dose IV administration

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Synthetic opioid agonist with high affinity for μ (mu)-opioid receptors in the CNS and peripheral tissues. Activates descending pain inhibitory pathways, inhibits nociceptive transmission in the dorsal horn, and modulates emotional response to pain. 100× more potent than morphine.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism via CYP3A4/CYP3A5 to inactive norfentanyl. IV/IN routes bypass first-pass.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites (<10% unchanged). Half-life 2–4 hours (single dose); context-sensitive half-life increases significantly with prolonged infusion.

adultDosing.notes: Slow IV push over 1–2 minutes. Onset 1–2 minutes IV. Titrate in increments of 25mcg assessing response. Reduce dose in elderly (12.5–25mcg increments).

adultDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat x1 after 10 minutes
adultDosing.notes: Use concentrated formulation (300mcg/mL or similar). Maximum 1mL per nostril per dose — divide between both nostrils. Onset 5–10 minutes. Use mucosal atomisation device (MAD).

adultDosing.frequency: Single dose; titrate with IV/IN as available
adultDosing.notes: IM onset 7–15 minutes. Less predictable than IV. Consider IV conversion when access established.

paediatricDosing.notes: Slow IV push over 1–2 minutes. Closely monitor respiratory rate and SpO₂. Maximum 50mcg per dose in children.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat x1 after 10 minutes
paediatricDosing.notes: Use concentrated formulation. Divide dose between nostrils (max 0.3–0.5mL per nostril in children). Use MAD device. Onset 5–10 minutes.

name: Fibrinogen Concentrate (RiaSTAP)
indications: Acquired hypofibrinogenaemia in major haemorrhage / trauma-induced coagulopathy
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to fibrinogen concentrate or any excipient
History of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) — fibrinogen itself not directly contraindicated but assess context
precautions: Risk of thromboembolic events — use minimum effective dose
Patients with history of thromboembolic disease — weigh risk vs benefit
Manufactured from human plasma — theoretical risk of viral transmission (extensively screened and heat-treated)
Monitor fibrinogen levels where available (target >1.5–2g/L in haemorrhage)
Reconstitute with supplied sterile water; administer within 8 hours of reconstitution
Warm product before administration

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Provides exogenous fibrinogen (Factor I), the essential substrate for thrombin-mediated clot formation. Fibrinogen is cleaved by thrombin to fibrin monomers, which polymerise to form the fibrin mesh of a stable clot. Restores clot integrity in acquired hypofibrinogenaemia (trauma, obstetric haemorrhage).
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Fibrinogen is a plasma protein that undergoes proteolytic degradation by thrombin (to fibrin) and plasmin (fibrinolysis) during normal haemostasis.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Metabolised and cleared through normal coagulation and fibrinolytic processes. Half-life of endogenous fibrinogen is approximately 3–5 days.

adultDosing.frequency: Stat; reassess and repeat based on fibrinogen levels and ongoing haemorrhage
adultDosing.maxDose: Per medical direction; repeat dosing based on fibrinogen levels
adultDosing.notes: Reconstitute 1g vial with 50mL warm sterile water. Administer by slow IV injection or infusion at no more than 5mL/minute. Target plasma fibrinogen >1.5g/L in major haemorrhage. Use as part of damage-control resuscitation protocol alongside TXA.
Early administration in PPH with suspected coagulopathy. Administer alongside uterotonic agents, oxytocin, and TXA as per major obstetric haemorrhage protocol.

paediatricDosing.notes: 70mg/kg IV (approximately 0.7mL/kg of reconstituted solution). Slow IV infusion. Monitor fibrinogen levels where possible.

name: Furosemide (Frusemide)
indications: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (ACPO) — reduces preload
Fluid overload with evidence of pulmonary congestion
Hyperkalaemia (in combination with other treatments — promotes renal potassium excretion)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to furosemide or sulfonamides
Anuria or severe renal failure (relative — may still be used in ACPO)
precautions: Ensure diagnosis of cardiogenic pulmonary oedema — furosemide will worsen non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (ARDS)
Monitor blood pressure — can cause significant hypotension, especially in hypovolaemic patients
GTN and non-invasive ventilation are first-line in ACPO; furosemide is adjunct
IV furosemide in ACPO acts partly as a vasodilator before diuresis begins (within 5–15 min)
Caution in patients on aminoglycosides — synergistic ototoxicity

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, preventing reabsorption of sodium, potassium, and chloride. Causes rapid and profound diuresis. Early vasodilatory effect (prostaglandin-mediated) reduces preload before diuresis begins.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism. Rapid oral and IV onset.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Primarily renal excretion unchanged. Onset IV: 5–15 minutes. Duration: 2–3 hours IV.

adultDosing.indication: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema — diuretic naïve (not on regular furosemide)
adultDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat 40 mg after 30–60 min if inadequate response
adultDosing.maxDose: 80 mg in acute setting without medical direction
adultDosing.notes: Administer slowly over ≥2 minutes. GTN and CPAP/BiPAP are first-line — furosemide is adjunct. If patient is hypotensive (SBP <90 mmHg), withhold furosemide. Monitor BP closely after administration.

adultDosing.indication: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema — patient on regular furosemide
adultDosing.dose: Double the patient's usual daily oral dose IV (minimum 40 mg, maximum 80 mg)
adultDosing.notes: Patients on regular furosemide require higher doses for acute effect due to diuretic resistance. Confirm usual dose from patient history or medication list.

paediatricDosing.notes: Use lower end of range (0.5 mg/kg) in pre-hospital setting. Monitor BP. Seek medical direction before administering to children.

name: Glucagon
indications: Severe hypoglycaemia when IV access is unavailable
Beta-blocker overdose / toxicity (refractory bradycardia and hypotension)
Oesophageal food bolus obstruction (smooth muscle relaxation — off-label)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to glucagon or lactose
precautions: Hypoglycaemia: patient must receive oral carbohydrates once conscious to prevent relapse
Limited efficacy in malnourished patients, starvation, or adrenal insufficiency — glycogen stores depleted
Vomiting common after glucagon — position patient in recovery position if unconscious
Beta-blocker overdose: doses much higher than hypoglycaemia doses required — 3–10mg IV
Short duration of action (15–30 minutes) — monitor blood glucose and redose or establish IV access

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Endogenous pancreatic hormone that activates hepatic glucagon receptors (Gs-coupled), stimulating adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis, rapidly increasing plasma glucose. At pharmacological doses, also increases cardiac contractility (positive inotrope/chronotrope) via cAMP, independent of β-adrenoreceptors — useful in beta-blocker/calcium channel blocker overdose.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Proteolytically degraded in plasma, liver, and kidneys.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Metabolites excreted renally. Half-life 8–18 minutes.

adultDosing.frequency: Stat; may repeat once after 15 minutes if no response
adultDosing.notes: Reconstitute with supplied diluent immediately before use. Inject into deltoid, outer thigh, or buttock. Onset 10–15 minutes IM. Turn patient on side (risk of vomiting). Once conscious, give oral carbohydrates. If no response after 15 minutes, consider IV glucose.

adultDosing.frequency: Over 2–3 minutes; may repeat 3mg bolus if no response; then infusion 2–5mg/hour
adultDosing.notes: High-dose glucagon for beta-blocker toxicity. Dilute in 5% dextrose. Give atropine and IV fluids concurrently. Onset within 2–5 minutes. Short duration — prepare infusion if effective.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Stat; may repeat once after 15 minutes
paediatricDosing.notes: For children <25kg use 0.5mg; ≥25kg use 1mg. Onset 10–15 minutes. Position on side. Give oral carbohydrates on recovery. Establish IV access as priority.

name: Glucose 10% IV
indications: Hypoglycaemia (blood glucose <4mmol/L with symptoms; <3mmol/L regardless of symptoms)
contraindications: Avoid in head injury and stroke as primary resuscitation fluid
precautions: Confirm hypoglycaemia with glucometer prior to administration where possible
Monitor blood glucose every 5–10 minutes after administration
Risk of hyperglycaemia — particularly in diabetics and patients with critical illness
Avoid extravasation — 10% glucose is mildly irritant to veins; use functioning IV line
Reassess BGL 10–15 minutes after treatment — rebound hypoglycaemia may occur (especially with long-acting insulin or sulphonylureas)
Thiamine deficiency (Wernicke's) — give thiamine before glucose in malnourished/alcoholic patients where thiamine available

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Directly provides glucose, the primary cellular energy substrate, for immediate uptake and metabolism. 10% concentration is mildly hypertonic (500 mOsm/L); used IV for hypoglycaemia when oral route is unavailable.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Phosphorylated to glucose-6-phosphate; metabolised via glycolysis to pyruvate → ATP. Insulin facilitates cellular uptake.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: CO2 and water produced by aerobic metabolism; excess urinary glucose if hyperglycaemia ensues.

adultDosing.frequency: Stat; reassess BGL after 5–10 minutes; repeat 100mL if BGL <5mmol/L
adultDosing.notes: Administer over 5–10 minutes. Reassess BGL and clinical status. Once BGL >5mmol/L and patient conscious, encourage oral intake. Monitor for rebound hypoglycaemia (long-acting insulin, sulphonylureas).

paediatricDosing.frequency: Stat; reassess BGL after 5–10 minutes; repeat if BGL remains <3mmol/L
paediatricDosing.notes: Administer 2mL/kg (0.2g/kg) over 5 minutes. IO route acceptable if IV unavailable. Neonates: 2.5mL/kg of 10% glucose. Reassess BGL and repeat if needed. Maintain BGL >4mmol/L in children.

name: Glucose Oral Gel
indications: Mild to moderate hypoglycaemia in a conscious patient who can swallow safely
Blood glucose <4mmol/L with symptoms in conscious cooperative patient
contraindications: Uncooperative patient or inability to swallow
precautions: Patient must have intact airway protective reflexes and be able to swallow safely
Apply gel to buccal mucosa (inside of cheeks) or under tongue if patient unable to actively swallow
Monitor BGL 10–15 minutes after administration
Follow with long-acting carbohydrate (food) once BGL normalised
If no improvement after 2 doses, escalate to IV glucose or glucagon
sideEffects: Aspiration (if given to impaired consciousness) — avoid
Dental caries (chronic use — not relevant in acute setting)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Rapidly absorbed glucose provides an immediate substrate for glycolysis and ATP production. Oral route relies on buccal/gastrointestinal absorption; only appropriate when patient can protect airway and swallow safely.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Absorbed directly from GI tract; undergoes glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation for ATP generation.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: CO2 and water via normal aerobic metabolism.

adultDosing.dose: 15–20g (one standard tube / sachet — approximately 37.5mL of 40% gel)
adultDosing.frequency: Repeat after 15 minutes if BGL still <4mmol/L; maximum 2–3 doses
adultDosing.maxDose: 45g (3 standard tubes) before escalating to IV route
adultDosing.notes: Squeeze into mouth — patient swallows or absorbs via buccal mucosa. Assess response in 10–15 minutes. Follow with sandwich, biscuits or other complex carbohydrate meal once BGL >5mmol/L.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Repeat after 15 minutes if BGL still <4mmol/L
paediatricDosing.notes: Apply to buccal mucosa. Ensure child is conscious and able to swallow. For neonates and infants, IV glucose preferred. Follow up with age-appropriate food once BGL normalised.

name: Glyceryl Trinitrate (GTN)
indications: Acute coronary syndrome — chest pain relief
Acute decompensated heart failure with hypertension (pulmonary oedema)
contraindications: Concurrent use of phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil/Viagra, tadalafil/Cialis, vardenafil) within 24–48 hours — risk of severe hypotension
Right ventricular infarction (inferior STEMI with RV involvement) — preload-dependent; GTN may cause severe hypotension
Severe bradycardia (<50 bpm) or tachycardia (>100 bpm) with cardiac compromise
precautions: Monitor BP before and after each dose — significant hypotension risk
Patients on antihypertensives — additive hypotension
Administer with patient seated or supine to prevent syncope
Right ventricular infarction: perform right-sided ECG (V4R) if inferior STEMI — avoid GTN if RV involvement suspected
Tolerance may develop with repeated dosing
Headache common — advise patient

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Organic nitrate prodrug converted to nitric oxide (NO) in vascular smooth muscle. NO activates guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation, causing venodilation (reducing preload), arterial dilation (reducing afterload), and coronary vasodilation (relieving vasospasm). Net effect reduces myocardial oxygen demand.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Rapid hepatic and blood vessel metabolism (organic nitrate reductase) to di- and mononitrates. Significant first-pass effect — sublingual/spray route bypasses this.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites. Half-life 1–4 minutes (IV); effective duration SL 20–30 minutes.

adultDosing.notes: Sublingual tablet under tongue or sublingual spray. Patient should be sitting or supine. Check BP before each dose — withhold if SBP <90mmHg. Onset 1–3 minutes. Duration 20–30 minutes.

adultDosing.indication: Acute decompensated heart failure / severe hypertension
adultDosing.frequency: Continuous IV infusion — titrate every 3–5 minutes
adultDosing.notes: IV GTN requires continuous BP monitoring. Dilute in normal saline or 5% dextrose. Use non-PVC giving set (GTN adsorbs to PVC). Titrate to symptom relief and SBP target.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Continuous IV infusion, titrate under medical direction
paediatricDosing.notes: GTN in paediatrics is rarely indicated prehospitally. IV infusion under specialist direction only. Monitor BP continuously.

name: Hartmann's Solution
indications: Haemorrhagic shock (damage control resuscitation — adjunct to blood products)
Septic shock (alongside antibiotics and source control)
contraindications: Hyperkalaemia (contains 5mmol/L potassium — avoid in severe hyperkalaemia)
Severe liver disease with lactic acidosis (lactate metabolism impaired — relative)
precautions: Large volume resuscitation can cause haemodilution, hypothermia, and dilutional coagulopathy
In traumatic haemorrhage: permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90mmHg) until haemorrhage control — avoid over-resuscitation with crystalloid
Hartmann's contains lactate — mild alkalising effect; avoid in severe metabolic alkalosis
Head injury: avoid hypotension (target SBP >90–110mmHg); however, limit crystalloid volume
Avoid in patients with known hyperkalaemia
Monitor electrolytes, fluid balance, and signs of fluid overload during large-volume infusions
sideEffects: Fluid overload / pulmonary oedema (with excess volumes)
Electrolyte disturbance (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia with large volumes)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Balanced crystalloid isotonic solution (Na 131 mmol/L, Cl 111 mmol/L, K 5 mmol/L, Ca 2 mmol/L, lactate 29 mmol/L). Lactate is metabolised to bicarbonate in the liver, buffering acidosis. Expands intravascular and interstitial fluid compartments to restore circulating volume.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Lactate is converted to bicarbonate (via pyruvate) in the liver and kidneys — requires hepatic function. Electrolytes integrate into normal fluid and electrolyte balance.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Excess fluid and electrolytes excreted by the kidneys via glomerular filtration and tubular regulation.

adultDosing.frequency: Reassess after each bolus; repeat as needed
adultDosing.maxDose: Per clinical response and haemorrhage control strategy
adultDosing.notes: Administer rapid IV bolus for haemodynamic compromise. Reassess BP, HR, and clinical response after each 500mL. In haemorrhagic shock, target permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90mmHg) until haemorrhage controlled. Warm fluids if possible to prevent hypothermia.

adultDosing.dose: 4mL/kg per % total body surface area burned (TBSA) over 24 hours (50% in first 8 hrs from time of burn)
adultDosing.notes: Parkland formula: 4mL × weight (kg) × %TBSA burned. Give first 50% in 8 hours from time of burn; remaining 50% over next 16 hours. Modified Parkland: 3–4mL/kg/%TBSA in some protocols.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Reassess after each bolus; repeat up to 3 boluses
paediatricDosing.notes: Administer 10mL/kg initial bolus, reassess, repeat up to 20mL/kg. In haemorrhagic trauma, limit crystalloid and transition to blood products early. In sepsis, 10mL/kg boluses reassessing after each.

name: Heparin Sodium
indications: Acute coronary syndrome (STEMI — peri-thrombolysis or PCI adjunct)
Prevention of thrombus formation (IV lines, catheters)
contraindications: Active major bleeding (not related to indication)
Hypersensitivity to heparin or pork products
Recent intracranial surgery or haemorrhagic stroke
precautions: Monitor for signs of bleeding at all sites
aPTT monitoring required for therapeutic anticoagulation (not typically available prehospitally)
Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) — avoid in patients with prior HIT
Caution in elderly, low body weight, or renal impairment
Do not give IM — risk of haematoma
Protamine sulphate reverses heparin anticoagulation
sideEffects: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT types I and II)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Indirect thrombin inhibitor: binds antithrombin III (AT-III), potentiating its inhibition of thrombin (Factor IIa) and Factor Xa by 1000×. Prevents clot propagation but does not lyse existing thrombus. Also inhibits Factors IXa, XIa, XIIa.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Metabolised by heparinase in the liver and reticuloendothelial system.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Partially excreted in urine unchanged. Half-life dose-dependent: 30–60 minutes for standard doses; increased in renal/hepatic impairment.

adultDosing.frequency: Single bolus; then infusion 12 units/kg/hour (max 1000 units/hour) under medical direction
adultDosing.maxDose: 5000 units initial bolus without weight-based calculation under time-critical protocols
adultDosing.notes: Weight-based: 60 units/kg IV bolus (max 4000 units) then 12 units/kg/hour infusion (max 1000 units/hour). In STEMI with thrombolysis, timing and dose as per specific thrombolytic protocol (e.g. tenecteplase). Adjust to aPTT 50–70 seconds.

paediatricDosing.dose: 75 units/kg IV loading dose over 10 minutes; maintenance 28 units/kg/hour (<1 year) or 20 units/kg/hour (>1 year)
paediatricDosing.maxDose: Per medical direction with aPTT monitoring
paediatricDosing.notes: Paediatric heparin therapy requires specialist direction and aPTT monitoring. For line flushes: 10 units/mL. Prehospital use restricted to medical direction.

name: Hydrocortisone
contraindications: Systemic fungal infections (relative — not relevant in acute emergency use)
precautions: In anaphylaxis: hydrocortisone is adjunct only — adrenaline is the primary treatment
Onset of action is 1–6 hours — does not provide immediate benefit in anaphylaxis; helps prevent biphasic reaction
Diabetes mellitus — may cause hyperglycaemia
Immunosuppression — caution in active infection
May mask signs of infection
Peptic ulcer disease — may worsen

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Synthetic glucocorticoid identical to endogenous cortisol. Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription: reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines, stabilises mast cells, inhibits phospholipase A2 (reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis), and restores vascular responsiveness to catecholamines in shock.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4 to inactive metabolites (tetrahydrocortisol, tetrahydrocortisone). Enterohepatic circulation of some metabolites.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion as conjugated metabolites. Half-life: plasma 8–12 minutes; biological effect 8–12 hours.

adultDosing.notes: Secondary to adrenaline in anaphylaxis. Aim to reduce biphasic reaction. IV preferred — dilute in 5mL water for injection; administer over 1–2 minutes.
Adjunct to bronchodilators. Onset of steroid effect 4–6 hours — continue bronchodilator therapy. Dilute in 5mL water for injection; administer slowly IV.
Give with IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline 1L rapidly). This is a life-threatening emergency — provide 100mg IM if IV not available initially.

paediatricDosing.notes: 4mg/kg IV/IM (max 200mg). Secondary to adrenaline in anaphylaxis. Slow IV injection.

name: Hydroxocobalamin (CYANOKIT)
indications: Smoke inhalation with suspected hydrogen cyanide exposure
Cyanide poisoning from industrial or fire exposure
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in suspected life-threatening cyanide poisoning
Known hypersensitivity to hydroxocobalamin or cyanocobalamin (relative)
precautions: Turns urine, skin, and mucous membranes deep red/crimson — inform patient and staff
May interfere with laboratory tests and pulse oximetry (false SpO₂ readings) for up to 24 hours
Contains cobalt — theoretical concern in patients with cobalt hypersensitivity
Blood pressure may increase transiently
Not a substitute for decontamination and airway management
In smoke inhalation with CO poisoning, treat CO as well (high-flow oxygen, consider hyperbaric if available)
sideEffects: Skin, mucosal membrane and urine discolouration (red/crimson) — expected and harmless

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Forms cyanocobalamin by avidly binding free cyanide ions (CN⁻) with greater affinity than cytochrome c oxidase. This sequesters cyanide and allows restoration of cellular aerobic respiration (oxidative phosphorylation) blocked by cyanide. Each hydroxocobalamin molecule binds one CN⁻ ion.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Cyanocobalamin formed in situ is a stable, non-toxic complex. Hydroxocobalamin is endogenous (Vitamin B12 precursor) and further metabolised in the liver.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of cyanocobalamin complex (turns urine red/pink — expected). Half-life of hydroxocobalamin approximately 26–31 hours.

adultDosing.indication: Cyanide poisoning / Smoke inhalation with suspected HCN
adultDosing.frequency: Stat over 15 minutes; may repeat 5g if no improvement
adultDosing.notes: Reconstitute 5g with 200mL normal saline. Infuse over 15 minutes. May repeat a second dose (5g) over 15 minutes if inadequate response, or third dose (5g) over 15–120 minutes under medical direction. Remove from smoke environment first. High-flow O₂ concurrently.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Stat over 15 minutes; may repeat once
paediatricDosing.notes: 70mg/kg IV infusion over 15 minutes. May repeat once if no improvement. Reconstitute proportionally. Maximum single dose 5g.

name: Hyoscine Butylbromide (Buscopan)
indications: Ureteric colic (renal colic) — smooth muscle relaxation
Gastrointestinal smooth muscle spasm (irritable bowel, bowel colic)
contraindications: Benign prostatic hyperplasia with urinary retention
precautions: Does not cross blood-brain barrier (quaternary ammonium compound) — minimal CNS effects
Monitor heart rate — can cause tachycardia
Caution in patients with underlying cardiac disease
IV administration may cause transient hypotension — inject slowly
Pain relief should not delay investigation of surgical cause (e.g. appendicitis)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Quaternary ammonium anticholinergic that does not cross the blood-brain barrier. Inhibits muscarinic receptors on smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal, biliary, and urinary tracts, reducing smooth muscle spasm. No significant CNS anticholinergic effects.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Poorly absorbed orally (~1%). IV onset: within minutes. IM onset: 15–30 minutes.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Bile and urine. Half-life ~5 hours.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat after 30 minutes; maximum 3 doses
adultDosing.notes: Administer over at least 1 minute IV. Rapid IV may cause tachycardia. May be combined with opioid analgesia for ureteric colic. Monitor heart rate and BP.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat after 30 minutes; maximum 3 doses
adultDosing.notes: IM into deltoid or anterolateral thigh. Onset 15–30 minutes.

paediatricDosing.notes: Seek medical direction before administering to children. Not recommended in children under 6 years old.

name: Hypertonic Saline 3% (HTS)
indications: Severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) with signs of transtentorial herniation
Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) with cerebral herniation
Hyponatraemic seizures unresponsive to standard treatment
precautions: Central pontine myelinolysis risk with overly rapid correction of chronic hyponatraemia — DO NOT use hypertonic saline for chronic asymptomatic hyponatraemia
For TBI/herniation: administer as bolus dose, not slow infusion
Monitor sodium levels where possible
Avoid in penetrating trauma without evidence of herniation
May cause phlebitis — use large vein or central access where possible
Osmolarity 1028 mOsm/L — monitor for volume overload in cardiac patients
sideEffects: Central pontine myelinolysis (with rapid correction of chronic hyponatraemia)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Hyperosmolar solution (approximately 1026 mOsm/L) that creates an osmotic gradient, drawing free water from the interstitium and cells into the intravascular space. Reduces cerebral oedema by shrinking brain parenchyma and lowering ICP. Also restores serum sodium in severe hyponatraemia.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Sodium and chloride are handled by normal electrolyte physiology — no metabolism of the salt itself.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Excess sodium and water excreted by the kidneys under aldosterone and ADH regulation.

adultDosing.indication: Severe TBI with signs of transtentorial herniation
adultDosing.frequency: Single bolus; may repeat once under medical direction
adultDosing.notes: Indicated for clinical signs of herniation: unequal pupils, posturing (decerebrate/decorticate), deteriorating GCS despite oxygenation and positioning. Elevate head of bed 30°. Simultaneously optimise oxygenation and ventilation.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat once if seizures continue
adultDosing.notes: For acute symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizures or reduced consciousness. Target is seizure cessation and clinical improvement, not normalisation of sodium. Specialist guidance for definitive management.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Single bolus; may repeat once under medical direction
paediatricDosing.notes: 3–5mL/kg IV/IO bolus. Indicated for clinical signs of herniation. Elevate head 30°. Optimise oxygenation.

name: Ibuprofen
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to ibuprofen, aspirin, or other NSAIDs
Active peptic ulcer or GI bleeding
precautions: Renal impairment — NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis; may precipitate acute kidney injury
Cardiovascular disease — increased risk of MI and stroke with prolonged use
GI protection: consider co-administering with food or a proton pump inhibitor in at-risk patients
Elderly — increased risk of GI bleeding, renal impairment, and cardiovascular events
Hypertension or fluid retention
Avoid in dehydrated patients
Pregnancy: avoid if possible, particularly third trimester

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Non-selective NSAID that reversibly inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes, blocking prostaglandin and thromboxane synthesis. Produces anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic effects. Unlike aspirin, inhibition is reversible.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via CYP2C9 to inactive hydroxylated and carboxylated metabolites.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites (predominantly) and glucuronide conjugates. Half-life 2 hours. Avoid in renal impairment.

adultDosing.maxDose: 1200mg per day (OTC); up to 2400mg per day under medical supervision
adultDosing.notes: Take with food or milk to reduce GI irritation. Onset 30–60 minutes PO. May combine with paracetamol for enhanced analgesia.

paediatricDosing.notes: 5–10mg/kg per dose. Give with food. Not recommended for children <3 months. Liquid formulation available (Nurofen for Children — 100mg/5mL). Maximum 4 doses in 24 hours.

name: Insulin (Actrapid / Soluble)
indications: Hyperkalaemia (glucose-insulin therapy for intracellular potassium shift)
Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) — careful IV dosing
precautions: Always confirm blood glucose before administration — hypoglycaemia is the primary risk
DKA: must have glucose ≥14mmol/L and IV fluid resuscitation prior to starting insulin; potassium must be ≥3.5mmol/L before insulin (risk of fatal hypokalaemia)
Hyperkalaemia: concurrent IV dextrose (50mL of 50% glucose) required to prevent hypoglycaemia; takes 15–30 minutes for potassium shift
Monitor BGL every 30–60 minutes during infusion
Insulin adsorbs to PVC infusion bags and tubing — flush line before use in DKA infusion
Potassium monitoring essential — insulin drives potassium into cells
IV Actrapid only — do not use long-acting insulin IV

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Endogenous pancreatic hormone (exogenous soluble insulin used therapeutically). Binds insulin receptors (tyrosine kinase), promoting cellular glucose uptake (via GLUT4 translocation), glycogenesis, lipogenesis, and protein synthesis. Inhibits glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. In DKA/HHS, restores glucose metabolism and inhibits ketogenesis.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Proteolytically degraded by insulinase in the liver (principal site, ~50% cleared), kidneys, and muscles.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Metabolites excreted renally. Endogenous insulin half-life 4–6 minutes; soluble insulin half-life 4–5 minutes IV, 60–90 minutes SC.

adultDosing.frequency: Continuous infusion; titrate per BGL response
adultDosing.maxDose: 0.1 units/kg/hour — do not exceed without medical direction
adultDosing.notes: Dilute in normal saline (50 units Actrapid in 50mL NaCl = 1 unit/mL). Commence AFTER adequate fluid resuscitation AND confirmed potassium ≥3.5mmol/L. Target BGL reduction of 2–3mmol/L per hour. Stop or reduce when BGL <14mmol/L and/or switch to dextrose-containing fluid.

adultDosing.dose: 10 units Actrapid IV bolus (with 50mL of 50% glucose simultaneously unless BGL >15mmol/L)
adultDosing.frequency: Stat; repeat under medical direction after 60 minutes if ECG changes persist
adultDosing.notes: Always give with dextrose (50mL of 50% glucose or 125mL of 20% glucose) unless BGL >15mmol/L. Monitor BGL every 30 minutes for 3 hours. Effect on potassium: onset 15–30 minutes, duration 2–6 hours.

paediatricDosing.notes: Paediatric DKA management is high-risk. Use 0.05 units/kg/hour initially in younger or more severe cases. Requires specialist guidance, careful BGL and electrolyte monitoring. Do not bolus insulin in paediatric DKA.

name: Ipratropium Bromide
indications: Severe acute asthma (adjunct to salbutamol — reduces hospital admission)
Bronchospasm not adequately responding to salbutamol alone
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to ipratropium, atropine, or related compounds
Hypersensitivity to soya lecithin or related food products (soya, peanut) in MDI formulation
precautions: Ipratropium is an adjunct to beta-2 agonists (salbutamol) — not first-line monotherapy for acute asthma
Avoid contact with eyes — may precipitate or worsen acute angle-closure glaucoma; use tightly-fitting nebuliser mask or mouthpiece
Caution in prostatic hypertrophy — may cause urinary retention
Narrow-angle glaucoma — use with caution; avoid nebuliser mist contact with eyes
Not for use as a rescue bronchodilator (salbutamol preferred)
sideEffects: Acute angle-closure glaucoma (if mist contacts eyes)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Quaternary ammonium anticholinergic agent. Competitively blocks muscarinic M3 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle and secretory glands, causing bronchodilation and reduced mucus secretion. Poor systemic absorption limits anticholinergic side effects.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Partially hydrolysed to inactive metabolites. Very low systemic bioavailability after inhalation (~7%).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal and faecal excretion of unchanged drug and metabolites. Half-life approximately 1.6 hours.

adultDosing.frequency: Every 20–30 minutes for 3 doses (combined with salbutamol); then 6 hourly
adultDosing.maxDose: 500mcg per dose; 2mg per 24 hours in acute setting
adultDosing.notes: Mix with salbutamol in the same nebuliser chamber. Use with a well-fitting mouthpiece or tightly-fitted mask to avoid eye contact. Onset 15 minutes; peak effect 1–2 hours.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Every 20 minutes for 3 doses combined with salbutamol; then 4–6 hourly
paediatricDosing.maxDose: 250mcg per dose in young children; 500mcg in older children
paediatricDosing.notes: Children <12 years: 250mcg (0.5mL of 0.5mg/mL or 0.025% solution) per dose. Mix with salbutamol in nebuliser. Use well-fitted paediatric mask — avoid mist contact with eyes.

name: Ketamine
precautions: Increases BP, HR and ICP — use with caution in hypertension, known intracranial pathology
Laryngospasm possible — have airway equipment available
Emergence reactions — consider co-administration of midazolam in full dissociative doses
Hypersalivation — consider glycopyrrolate or atropine pre-treatment
Hepatic impairment — reduce dose
Pregnancy — use only if clearly needed
High-dose IV must only be given by credentialled paramedic

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Dissociative anaesthetic and analgesic. Non-competitive NMDA (N-methyl-D-aspartate) glutamate receptor antagonist, blocking calcium influx and central sensitisation. Preserves airway reflexes and respiratory drive at analgesic doses. Sympathomimetic effect (catecholamine release) maintains or increases BP and HR. Also has opioid receptor and sodium channel activity.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism via CYP3A4/CYP2B6 to norketamine (active, ~30% potency) and hydroxynorketamine (inactive).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites. Half-life: distribution 7–15 minutes; elimination 2–3 hours.

adultDosing.notes: Dilute to 1mg/mL. Administer slowly over 5–10 minutes to reduce side effects. Typical adult dose 10–20mg IV.

adultDosing.frequency: Single dose, may repeat once after 15 minutes
adultDosing.notes: Onset 3–5 minutes IM. Useful when IV access not available.
Give rapidly IV. Onset 30–60 seconds. Must be given by credentialled paramedic with RSI authorisation.
Dissociative dose. Onset 3–5 minutes IM. Airway management equipment must be immediately available.

paediatricDosing.notes: Dilute to 1mg/mL. Give slowly over 5–10 minutes.
Onset 3–5 minutes IM.
Dissociative dose in children. Airway management equipment must be available.

name: Levetiracetam
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to levetiracetam or pyrrolidone derivatives
precautions: Renal impairment — dose reduction required
Behavioural disturbances possible (agitation, aggression)
Pregnancy — use with caution, benefit vs risk
Administer IV no faster than recommended rate to avoid hypotension and bradycardia
Monitor neurological status throughout infusion

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Anticonvulsant with unique mechanism: binds synaptic vesicle protein SV2A, modulating vesicular neurotransmitter release and synchronisation of neuronal firing. Does not act on sodium channels or GABA receptors directly. Broad-spectrum activity against focal and generalised seizures.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism (~24%); primarily enzymatic hydrolysis of acetamide group in blood (not CYP-dependent).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: ~66% excreted unchanged in urine; remainder as inactive metabolite. Half-life 6–8 hours; prolonged in renal impairment (dose adjustment required).

adultDosing.notes: Dilute in 100mL NaCl 0.9% or dextrose 5%. Infuse over 15 minutes. Second-line after benzodiazepines.

paediatricDosing.notes: Dilute in appropriate volume of NaCl 0.9%. Infuse over 15 minutes. Second-line after benzodiazepines. Closely monitor for hypotension.

name: Lignocaine 1%
indications: Ventricular arrhythmias — VT/VF refractory to defibrillation (second-line)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to amide local anaesthetics
Severe sinoatrial, AV or intraventricular heart block (without pacemaker)
precautions: Do not inject intravascularly — aspirate before injection
Systemic toxicity risk with large doses or inadvertent intravascular injection (LAST — Local Anaesthetic Systemic Toxicity)
Hepatic impairment — reduced clearance
Pregnancy — use with caution
Epilepsy — may lower seizure threshold at toxic levels
Cardiac failure or shock — reduce dose
sideEffects: CNS toxicity (tinnitus, perioral numbness, confusion, seizures) — LAST
Cardiovascular toxicity (bradycardia, hypotension, cardiac arrest) — LAST
Local pain and burning on injection

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Amide local anaesthetic and Class Ib antiarrhythmic. Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in the inactivated state, preventing action potential propagation in peripheral nerves (anaesthesia) and suppressing ventricular ectopic foci (antiarrhythmic at IV doses). Preferentially binds depolarised/rapidly firing cells.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensive hepatic first-pass via CYP3A4/CYP1A2 to active metabolites MEGX and GX (potential for toxicity). Minimal first-pass for local infiltration.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites; <10% unchanged. Half-life 1.5–2 hours (may be prolonged in heart failure/hepatic disease).

adultDosing.notes: 1% solution = 10mg/mL. Always aspirate before injecting. Wait 3–5 minutes for full effect.

adultDosing.indication: IO cavity anaesthesia prior to infusion
adultDosing.frequency: Single dose, slow push over 1–2 minutes
adultDosing.notes: Flush IO with 5mL saline first, then inject lignocaine slowly, then flush with saline. Reduces IO infusion pain.
Bolus IV slowly over 2–3 minutes. Second-line antiarrhythmic if amiodarone unavailable.

paediatricDosing.notes: 1% = 10mg/mL. Always aspirate before injecting. Warm solution to reduce pain.

paediatricDosing.indication: IO cavity anaesthesia prior to infusion
paediatricDosing.notes: Inject slowly over 1–2 minutes then flush with saline.

name: Loperamide
indications: Acute gastroenteritis with significant diarrhoea affecting work capacity
contraindications: Bloody diarrhoea or dysentery (may be infective colitis — risk of toxic megacolon)
Acute diarrhoea with high fever and systemic toxicity
Children under 12 years (OTC dosing — seek medical direction)
precautions: Do not use if diarrhoea is bloody or accompanied by significant fever — may indicate infective colitis
Ensure adequate oral hydration — diarrhoea replacement is the primary treatment
High doses above recommended may cause QT prolongation and cardiac arrhythmias — do not exceed
Not recommended in severe liver disease
Does not cross blood-brain barrier at therapeutic doses — minimal CNS effects

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Peripheral μ-opioid receptor agonist acting on the gut wall. Decreases intestinal peristalsis and increases anal sphincter tone. Increases water and electrolyte absorption from intestinal lumen. Does not cross blood-brain barrier at therapeutic doses.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism. Onset 1–2 hours.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Bile and faecal excretion. Half-life 9–14 hours.

adultDosing.dose: 4 mg initially, then 2 mg after each unformed stool
adultDosing.frequency: As required after each loose stool
adultDosing.maxDose: 8 mg per day (OTC supply without medical direction); up to 16 mg per day with medical direction
adultDosing.notes: Initial dose 4 mg (2 capsules), then 2 mg after each loose stool. Do not exceed 8 mg/day without medical direction. If no improvement after 48 hours, reassess for infective cause. Maintain oral hydration throughout.

name: Loratadine
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to loratadine or desloratadine
precautions: Hepatic impairment — reduce dosing frequency (alternate days)
Renal impairment — reduce dosing frequency
Pregnancy — avoid in first trimester if possible
NOT a substitute for adrenaline in anaphylaxis
Minimal sedation but caution with alcohol or other CNS depressants

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Second-generation selective peripheral H1 histamine receptor antagonist. Blocks histamine-mediated increase in capillary permeability, smooth muscle contraction, and itch/sneezing. Low CNS penetration due to P-glycoprotein efflux minimises sedation.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic first-pass via CYP3A4 and CYP2D6 to active metabolite desloratadine.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal and faecal excretion of metabolites. Half-life 8–12 hours (loratadine), 27 hours (desloratadine).

adultDosing.indication: Allergic rhinitis / urticaria / mild allergic reaction
adultDosing.notes: Onset 1–3 hours. Can be given without regard to food.

paediatricDosing.notes: Children 2–12 years weighing <30kg: 5mg daily. Use syrup if available.
Children ≥30kg or >12 years: adult dose applies.

name: Magnesium Sulphate
indications: Eclampsia and severe pre-eclampsia (seizure prevention and treatment)
Severe acute asthma refractory to bronchodilators
precautions: Monitor for signs of magnesium toxicity: loss of patellar reflexes, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest
Have calcium gluconate 10% ready as antidote (10mL IV)
Renal impairment — reduced excretion, risk of toxicity
Continuous cardiac monitoring and BP monitoring during infusion
Neonates born to mothers given magnesium may have respiratory depression
sideEffects: Loss of deep tendon reflexes (early toxicity sign)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Magnesium is a physiological calcium antagonist and NMDA receptor blocker. Inhibits catecholamine release, reduces smooth muscle and myocardial excitability, and prevents seizure activity. In eclampsia: central anticonvulsant via NMDA antagonism. In asthma: bronchodilation via smooth muscle relaxation. In torsades de pointes: stabilises cardiac membrane.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Magnesium ions are not metabolised. Distribute into bone, intracellular fluid, and soft tissues.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Almost entirely renally excreted; hypermagnesaemia risk in renal impairment. Close monitoring of serum levels, reflexes, and respiratory rate required.
