adultDosing.notes: Dilute 4g (8mL of 50%) in 100mL NaCl 0.9%. Infuse over 15–20 minutes. Monitor reflexes, respiration and BP. Antidote: calcium gluconate 10% 10mL IV.
Dilute in 100mL NaCl 0.9% or 5% dextrose. Infuse over 20 minutes. Monitor for hypotension.

adultDosing.frequency: Rapid IV push over 1–2 minutes (pulseless); over 10 minutes (with pulse)
adultDosing.notes: If pulseless VT/VF: 2g IV rapid bolus. If stable: 2g over 10 minutes. Continuous cardiac monitoring essential.

paediatricDosing.notes: Dilute in 100mL NaCl 0.9%. Infuse over 20 minutes. Monitor BP and respiratory status.
Monitor for respiratory depression. Have calcium gluconate available.

name: Metaraminol Tartrate
indications: Acute hypotension not responsive to fluid resuscitation
precautions: Extravasation causes tissue necrosis — use central or large peripheral IV
Hypertension risk — monitor BP closely
Cardiac arrhythmias — may provoke reflex bradycardia
Coronary artery disease — increased myocardial oxygen demand
Diabetes mellitus — may cause hyperglycaemia
Pregnancy — reduces uteroplacental blood flow
Volume depletion — correct hypovolaemia concurrently

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Sympathomimetic amine with predominant α1-adrenoreceptor agonist activity, causing arteriolar vasoconstriction and increased systemic vascular resistance (SVR). Some indirect activity (displaces noradrenaline from vesicles). Increases MAP with minimal direct cardiac chronotropy (reflex bradycardia may occur from baroreceptor activation).
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Partially metabolised by MAO; remainder excreted unchanged. Less efficiently metabolised than catecholamines.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of unchanged drug and metabolites. Duration of action 20–60 minutes (longer than adrenaline).

adultDosing.frequency: Titrated, repeat every 2–5 minutes to effect
adultDosing.maxDose: 10mg per episode (medical consultation for higher doses)
adultDosing.notes: Dilute to 0.5mg/mL for IV bolus. Administer slowly over 1–2 minutes. Monitor BP every 2 minutes. Target systolic BP 90–100mmHg.
Infusion for sustained vasopressor support. Mix 15–100mg in 500mL NaCl 0.9%. Titrate to maintain systolic >90mmHg.

paediatricDosing.notes: Dilute to 0.1mg/mL before use in children. Titrate carefully to BP response. Use with caution in paediatric patients.

name: Methoxyflurane
indications: Moderate to severe acute pain (trauma, procedural, musculoskeletal)
Short-term analgesia where IV access not yet established
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity or previous adverse reaction to methoxyflurane or halogenated anaesthetics
Personal or family history of malignant hyperthermia
Altered level of consciousness (unable to self-administer)
Susceptibility to hepatotoxicity from halogenated agents
precautions: Must be patient-controlled (self-administered) — do not force use in uncooperative patients
Maximum 6mL per day, maximum 15mL per week
Repeated exposure increases risk of renal and hepatic toxicity
Use in well-ventilated area — occupational exposure risk to clinicians
Drowsiness may impair driving and coordination
Head injury — may impair neurological assessment
Pregnancy — limited data; use with caution
Children <6 years: not recommended
sideEffects: Renal toxicity (prolonged or repeated high-dose use)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Inhaled halogenated volatile anaesthetic analgesic. Precise mechanism uncertain; likely modulates NMDA, GABA-A, and glycine receptors, and voltage-gated sodium/calcium channels at analgesic sub-anaesthetic doses. Provides rapid onset analgesia (within 6–10 breaths).
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensive pulmonary and hepatic metabolism (~70%) via CYP2E1 to free fluoride ions and other metabolites (difluoroacetic acid, oxalic acid). High-dose or prolonged use risks nephrotoxicity from fluoride accumulation — analgesic doses are designed to minimise this.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Exhaled unchanged (~30%) and metabolites renally excreted. Fluoride ions excreted in urine.

adultDosing.notes: Pour 3mL into Penthrox inhaler. Patient self-administers by inhaling through mouthpiece as needed for pain. Onset 6–10 breaths. Activate carbon chamber on inhaler to reduce clinician exposure.

paediatricDosing.notes: Suitable for children ≥6 years who can self-administer. Must be patient-controlled. Monitor for excessive drowsiness.

name: Metoclopramide
indications: Nausea and vomiting associated with opioid administration
contraindications: History of tardive dyskinesia or extrapyramidal disorders
precautions: Extrapyramidal side effects (acute dystonic reaction) — especially in young females and children
Treat dystonic reactions with benztropine or diphenhydramine
Avoid in Parkinson's disease — dopamine antagonism
Renal impairment — reduce dose
Elderly — increased risk of extrapyramidal effects
Avoid repeat dosing beyond 5 days

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Central and peripheral dopamine D2 receptor antagonist. Centrally, blocks the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ) producing antiemetic effect. Peripherally, enhances gastric motility and gastric emptying (prokinetic) by sensitising gut smooth muscle to acetylcholine and blocking inhibitory dopamine. Also weak 5-HT3 antagonist.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via CYP2D6 (N-deethylation, sulfate conjugation).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Primarily renal excretion of unchanged drug (approximately 85%) and metabolites. Half-life 5–6 hours; prolonged in renal failure.

adultDosing.notes: Administer slowly over 2–3 minutes IV. Onset 1–3 minutes.
Onset 10–15 minutes IM.

paediatricDosing.notes: Administer slowly over 2–3 minutes. High risk of acute dystonic reactions in children and young adults — use with caution. Consider ondansetron as preferred antiemetic in paediatrics.

name: Metoprolol
indications: Rate control in atrial fibrillation / atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response
Hypertensive urgency with associated tachycardia (not hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage)
Adjunct in STEMI/ACS — reduce myocardial oxygen demand (after stabilisation)
contraindications: Second or third degree AV block (without pacemaker)
Known hypersensitivity to metoprolol or other beta-blockers
Cocaine-induced tachycardia (beta-blockers contraindicated — risk of unopposed alpha)
precautions: Assess for signs of heart failure before administering — may worsen acutely decompensated HF
Monitor ECG and blood pressure continuously after IV administration
Bradycardia and AV block may occur — atropine should be available
Beta-blockers mask tachycardia as a sign of hypoglycaemia
Abrupt withdrawal after chronic use may precipitate rebound angina
Drug interactions: verapamil and diltiazem increase risk of bradycardia and AV block

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Selective competitive antagonist at β1-adrenoceptors (cardiac). Reduces heart rate, myocardial contractility, and AV nodal conduction velocity. At high doses, some β2 antagonism (bronchospasm risk).
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism (bioavailability ~50% oral). IV onset 1–5 minutes.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites. Plasma half-life 3–7 hours.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat every 5 minutes to a total of 3 doses
adultDosing.notes: Administer slowly over 2–5 minutes. Monitor ECG and BP continuously. Do not administer if HR <60, SBP <100, PR >0.24 sec, or signs of heart failure. Stop if rate controlled. Have atropine available.

adultDosing.indication: AF / SVT rate control — oral (stable patients)
adultDosing.notes: Oral route for haemodynamically stable patients. Slower onset (1–2 hours). Use IV route for urgent rate control.

paediatricDosing.notes: Seek medical direction before administering to children. Monitor ECG and BP. Not first-line in paediatric SVT (adenosine preferred).

name: Midazolam
contraindications: Acute alcohol intoxication with CNS depression
precautions: Respiratory depression — have flumazenil and airway equipment available
Hypotension — particularly in hypovolaemic or elderly patients
Elderly — significantly reduce dose (half adult dose)
Hepatic impairment — reduced metabolism
Head injury — sedation may mask neurological deterioration
Concurrent opioid use increases respiratory depression risk
IN administration requires concentrated formulation (5mg/mL)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Short-acting benzodiazepine. Positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors — binds benzodiazepine site, increasing frequency of chloride channel opening in response to GABA, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission. Produces anxiolysis, sedation, amnesia, muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effect.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4 to active 1-hydroxymidazolam (glucuronidated to active 1-hydroxymidazolam glucuronide — renally cleared, accumulates in renal failure). Significant first-pass effect oral/buccal.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of glucuronide metabolites. Half-life 1.5–2.5 hours; prolonged in hepatic disease, elderly, and renal failure (active metabolite).

adultDosing.notes: Administer slowly over 1–2 minutes. Titrate to seizure cessation. Monitor airway and breathing.

adultDosing.indication: Active seizure / status epilepticus (no IV access)
adultDosing.notes: Use concentrated 5mg/mL formulation for IM. Onset 5–10 minutes.
Use 5mg/mL concentrated solution. Max 1mL per nostril. Onset 5–10 minutes.
Reduce dose in elderly (start 0.5–1mg). Concurrent opioid use — reduce dose by 30–50%.

paediatricDosing.notes: Administer slowly. Monitor airway and SpO2.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Single dose, may repeat once after 10 minutes
paediatricDosing.notes: Use concentrated formulation. Onset 5–10 minutes.
Use 5mg/mL concentrated solution. Administer 0.5mL per nostril at a time. Onset 5–10 minutes.
Administer into buccal space (cheek). Onset 5–10 minutes. Use liquid formulation.

name: Morphine + Midazolam Combination
indications: Palliative and end-of-life comfort care (symptom management)
Procedural sedation and analgesia in remote/FIFO settings
Severe pain with concurrent anxiety or agitation
Rescue analgesia for refractory pain with anxiety component
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to morphine or benzodiazepines
Head injury with altered GCS — use with extreme caution (may mask deterioration)
precautions: Synergistic respiratory depression — reduce individual doses when combining
Continuous monitoring of SpO2 and respiratory rate essential
Naloxone must be available (reverses morphine; midazolam reversed by flumazenil)
Elderly — significantly reduce doses
Hypotension risk — especially in volume-depleted patients
Must have IV access and airway management capability available

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Combination of μ-opioid receptor agonist (morphine) and GABA-A positive allosteric modulator (midazolam), providing synergistic analgesia, sedation, and anxiolysis. Used in palliative/end-of-life care for symptom management.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Morphine: hepatic glucuronidation to M6G (active) and M3G (inactive). Midazolam: CYP3A4 to 1-hydroxymidazolam (active). Both require dose reduction in renal/hepatic impairment.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Both drugs excreted renally (metabolites). Morphine half-life 2–4 hours; midazolam half-life 1.5–2.5 hours.

adultDosing.indication: Procedural sedation and analgesia / palliative symptom management
adultDosing.frequency: Titrated; may repeat reduced doses after 5–10 minutes
adultDosing.notes: Administer each agent separately. Give morphine first, assess, then give midazolam if required. Reduce doses by 50% in elderly or if hypotensive. Naloxone available.
Can be combined in same syringe for SC administration. Follow palliative care plan. Titrate to comfort.

paediatricDosing.notes: Administer morphine first, assess after 3–5 minutes, then add midazolam if required. Closely monitor airway and SpO2.

name: Morphine Sulphate
indications: Pulmonary oedema — adjunct (reduces preload and anxiety)
contraindications: Acute abdomen (relative — may mask symptoms; balance with analgesia need)
Head injury with severely raised ICP (relative)
precautions: Respiratory depression — have naloxone immediately available
Hypotension — reduce dose in hypovolaemia
Elderly — reduce dose; prolonged effect
Renal impairment — active metabolite accumulates (morphine-6-glucuronide)
Hepatic impairment — increased bioavailability and reduced clearance
Head injury — may mask neurological deterioration
Concurrent CNS depressants — additive depression
Nausea — consider antiemetic (ondansetron, metoclopramide)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Prototype opioid analgesic. Agonist at μ (mu), κ (kappa), and δ (delta) opioid receptors in CNS (PAG, dorsal horn), peripheral tissues, and GI tract. Inhibits ascending pain transmission, activates descending inhibitory pathways, alters emotional response to pain, and produces euphoria and respiratory depression at higher doses.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation (UGT2B7) to morphine-6-glucuronide (M6G — 6× more potent analgesic) and morphine-3-glucuronide (M3G — neuroexcitatory). Minimal CYP involvement.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of glucuronides; M6G accumulates in renal failure. Half-life 2–4 hours.

adultDosing.notes: Dilute to 1mg/mL. Administer slowly over 2–3 minutes. Titrate to pain score <4. Reduce dose in elderly (start 1–2.5mg).

adultDosing.indication: Moderate to severe pain (no IV access)
adultDosing.notes: Onset 15–30 minutes IM/SC.
Note: Some guidelines note that morphine may interfere with P2Y12 inhibitor absorption; clinical judgement required. Preferred in STEMI with severe pain.

paediatricDosing.notes: Dilute to 0.1–1mg/mL. Administer slowly. Monitor SpO2 and respiratory rate. Naloxone available.

paediatricDosing.indication: Moderate to severe pain (no IV access)
paediatricDosing.notes: Onset 15–30 minutes. Monitor closely.

name: Naloxone
indications: Opioid overdose — reversal of respiratory depression and sedation
precautions: Short duration of action (30–90 minutes) — repeat dosing or infusion may be required with long-acting opioids (e.g., methadone, slow-release opioids)
Abrupt opioid reversal may precipitate acute withdrawal (agitation, vomiting, tachycardia, hypertension, pulmonary oedema in opioid-dependent patients)
Titrate dose carefully in opioid-dependent patients — use lowest effective dose
Monitor for re-narcotisation after effect wears off
Does NOT reverse non-opioid sedation (benzodiazepines, alcohol, etc.)
Pregnancy — safe to use in life-threatening maternal opioid overdose
sideEffects: Acute opioid withdrawal (agitation, vomiting, diaphoresis, tachycardia)
Pulmonary oedema (rare — large doses in opioid-dependent)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at μ, κ, and δ opioid receptors with high affinity, rapidly displacing opioids from receptors. Reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression, sedation, miosis, and analgesia. No intrinsic agonist activity (pure antagonist). Duration shorter than most opioids — repeated dosing may be needed.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation to naloxone-3-glucuronide (inactive). Significant first-pass effect — IV/IM/IN preferred over oral.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of glucuronide metabolite. Half-life 30–90 minutes (IV); shorter than most opioids.

adultDosing.frequency: Every 2–3 minutes until respiratory rate adequate
adultDosing.notes: Start low (0.1–0.4mg) and titrate in opioid-dependent patients to avoid precipitating withdrawal. Full reversal dose 0.4–2mg. Onset 1–2 minutes IV.
Onset 3–5 minutes IM. Lateral thigh preferred site.
Use concentrated formulation (2mg/mL). Administer 0.5–1mL per nostril. Onset 5–10 minutes. Narcan nasal spray available for lay responder use.

paediatricDosing.notes: Neonates (opioid-exposed): 0.01mg/kg IV/IM. Titrate carefully. Monitor for re-sedation.
Use IM in children where IV not accessible. Onset 3–5 minutes.

name: Noradrenaline
indications: Vasodilatory shock refractory to fluid resuscitation (septic shock, neurogenic shock)
Hypotension refractory to other vasopressors or fluids
contraindications: Hypovolaemic shock without adequate volume resuscitation (relative — address volume first)
Hypersensitivity to noradrenaline or sodium metabisulfite (excipient)
precautions: Extravasation causes severe tissue necrosis — administer via central venous catheter whenever possible
Peripheral IV use in emergencies only — use large bore, proximal IV and monitor site closely
Hypertension — monitor BP every 2–5 minutes
Reflex bradycardia
Peripheral ischaemia — monitor extremity perfusion
Cardiac arrhythmias possible
Volume depletion should be corrected concurrently
Pregnancy — reduces uteroplacental flow
sideEffects: Renal impairment (reduced renal perfusion at high doses)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Endogenous catecholamine and potent α1 and α2-adrenoreceptor agonist with moderate β1 activity and minimal β2 activity. Primarily causes intense vasoconstriction increasing SVR and MAP. Mild positive inotropy. Reflex bradycardia may occur. Drug of choice for vasodilatory (distributive/septic) shock.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Metabolised by MAO and COMT in the liver, kidneys, and at the synapse to normetanephrine and VMA (inactive).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites. Plasma half-life 2–3 minutes (continuous infusion required).

adultDosing.notes: Dilute in 5% glucose or NaCl 0.9% (do not dilute in alkaline solutions). Typical concentration 4mg in 250mL (16mcg/mL). Central line preferred. Titrate to MAP >=65mmHg. Use FIFO/remote only under medical direction.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Continuous infusion, titrated to age-appropriate BP
paediatricDosing.notes: Dilute appropriately. Central access strongly preferred. Dose and titration under paediatric/specialist guidance. Monitor extremity perfusion.

name: Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl)
indications: IO flush following IO drug administration
contraindications: Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis (with large volumes — use Hartmann's preferentially)
precautions: Large volumes of normal saline cause hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis — consider Hartmann's as preferred resuscitation fluid for most indications
Fluid overload — monitor for signs of pulmonary oedema
Head injury: avoid hypotonic fluids; NS is preferred in TBI for this reason over Hartmann's by some protocols
Normal saline preferred diluent for many medications (check drug compatibility)
Monitor electrolytes with large-volume infusions

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Isotonic crystalloid (154 mmol/L Na, 154 mmol/L Cl; 308 mOsm/L). Expands intravascular and interstitial fluid compartments. Large volumes can cause hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis. Used for volume resuscitation and as a vehicle for drug infusions.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Sodium and chloride ions participate in normal electrolyte physiology — no metabolism of the ionic components.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Excess fluid and electrolytes regulated and excreted renally under aldosterone and ADH control.

adultDosing.frequency: Reassess after each bolus; repeat as clinically indicated
adultDosing.maxDose: Per clinical response; avoid >2L before considering alternative strategies or blood products
adultDosing.notes: Rapid IV bolus. Reassess HR, BP, and clinical response after each bolus. In haemorrhagic shock: permissive hypotension strategy (SBP 80–90mmHg) until haemorrhage control.
Flush IV lines after drug administration to ensure full drug delivery. IO flush: 10mL with push of 5–10mL saline to clear dead space.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Reassess after each bolus; repeat as needed
paediatricDosing.maxDose: 60mL/kg total before considering blood products or medical direction
paediatricDosing.notes: Initial bolus 10mL/kg; reassess and repeat. In sepsis, give 10mL/kg boluses monitoring for fluid overload. In traumatic haemorrhage, limit crystalloid and use blood products early.
Use 3–5mL normal saline flush after IV/IO drug administration in children.

name: Olanzapine Oral Dispersible (Zydis)
indications: Acute agitation associated with schizophrenia or bipolar disorder
contraindications: Concurrent parenteral benzodiazepine use (significantly increased risk of respiratory depression and death when IM olanzapine + IM benzodiazepine — avoid combination within 1 hour)
precautions: Do NOT administer IM olanzapine concurrently with IM/IV benzodiazepine — high risk of fatal respiratory depression (wait at least 1 hour)
Oral dispersible tablet (ODT) is safer than IM when benzodiazepine co-administration anticipated
QT prolongation — use with caution with other QT-prolonging drugs
Hypotension — especially with benzodiazepines or opioids
Elderly with dementia — increased mortality risk
Seizure threshold lowering
Parkinson's disease — worsens symptoms
Respiratory depression risk in COAD or respiratory disease
sideEffects: Extrapyramidal effects (less common than typical antipsychotics)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Second-generation (atypical) antipsychotic. Antagonises dopamine D1–D4 receptors (particularly D2), serotonin 5-HT2A/2C, histamine H1, muscarinic, and α1-adrenoreceptors. Produces sedation, reduces psychomotor agitation, and has antiemetic properties.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via CYP1A2 and CYP2D6 (glucuronidation and N-demethylation) to inactive metabolites.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal (57%) and faecal (30%) excretion of metabolites. Half-life 30 hours; prolonged in elderly and in hepatic impairment.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat 5mg after 2 hours if required
adultDosing.notes: Place tablet on tongue — dissolves rapidly without water. Avoid IM benzodiazepine within 1 hour. Onset 15–45 minutes. Preferred over IM when IV/IM benzodiazepine anticipated.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat once after 2 hours
paediatricDosing.notes: Use in adolescents >=13 years under medical direction. Monitor for hypotension and sedation.

name: Ondansetron
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to ondansetron or other 5-HT3 antagonists
precautions: QT prolongation — avoid in patients with known prolonged QT or on other QT-prolonging drugs
Serotonin syndrome risk when combined with other serotonergic agents
Hepatic impairment — maximum 8mg/day
Bowel obstruction — may mask symptoms of obstruction with reduced motility
ECG monitoring recommended IV in high-risk patients

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Selective 5-HT3 (serotonin type 3) receptor antagonist. Blocks 5-HT3 receptors both peripherally (vagal afferents in the GI tract) and centrally (chemoreceptor trigger zone), preventing serotonin-induced nausea and vomiting signalling.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4, CYP1A2, and CYP2D6 to inactive hydroxylated and glucuronide conjugates.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites (<5% unchanged). Half-life 3–5 hours; prolonged in hepatic impairment.

adultDosing.notes: Administer IV over 2–5 minutes (slow push). For IV infusion, dilute in 50mL NaCl 0.9% and give over 15 minutes. Max single IV dose 16mg (QT risk).
Onset 30–60 minutes IM.
ODT dissolves on tongue. Onset 30–60 minutes.

paediatricDosing.maxDose: 4mg per dose (<12 years); 8mg per dose (>=12 years)
paediatricDosing.notes: Administer slowly over 2–5 minutes. Monitor for QT prolongation.
Use ODT formulation if available. Preferred antiemetic in paediatrics over metoclopramide.

name: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu)
indications: Treatment of confirmed or highly suspected influenza — symptomatic patients (most effective if started within 48 hours of symptom onset)
Post-exposure prophylaxis in high-risk contacts of confirmed influenza case (mine site camp outbreak)
Treatment in hospitalised or seriously ill patients regardless of symptom duration
precautions: Most effective when started within 48 hours of symptom onset — do not delay
Beneficial even after 48 hours in hospitalised or severely ill patients
Renal impairment: dose reduction required for eGFR <60 mL/min
Monitor for neuropsychiatric events in children and adolescents (rare — hallucinations, abnormal behaviour)
Does not replace vaccination — encourage seasonal influenza vaccination
In camp outbreaks: consult site medical officer re mass prophylaxis protocol
Not effective against non-influenza respiratory illnesses (COVID-19, rhinovirus, etc.)
sideEffects: Nausea and vomiting (most common — take with food)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Selective inhibitor of influenza A and B neuraminidase — prevents cleavage of sialic acid residues, inhibiting viral release from infected cells and reducing viral spread.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Oral prodrug converted by hepatic esterases to active carboxylate metabolite. Oral bioavailability ~75% as active metabolite.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion unchanged. Half-life 6–10 hours. Duration of treatment: 5 days.

adultDosing.notes: Start within 48 hours of symptom onset for maximum benefit. Take with food to reduce nausea. Duration: 5 days. For severe/hospitalised: 75 mg twice daily for 10 days or until clinically improved.

adultDosing.indication: Post-exposure prophylaxis (high-risk contacts in camp outbreak)
adultDosing.notes: Start within 48 hours of exposure. Duration: 10 days (or up to 6 weeks during community outbreak). Use only for high-risk contacts (immunocompromised, elderly, comorbid) or per mass prophylaxis direction from site medical officer.

paediatricDosing.dose: Weight-based: <15 kg = 30 mg BD; 15–23 kg = 45 mg BD; 24–40 kg = 60 mg BD; >40 kg = 75 mg BD
paediatricDosing.notes: Oral suspension available for children. Start within 48 hours. Duration 5 days.

name: Oxygen
indications: Trauma with haemorrhage or respiratory compromise
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in emergency/life-threatening conditions
Routine high-flow oxygen in non-hypoxic patients with COPD — risk of hypercapnic respiratory failure (use titrated therapy)
precautions: COPD with chronic hypercapnia — titrate to SpO2 88–92% to avoid hypercapnic drive suppression
Premature neonates — risk of retinopathy of prematurity with high FiO2; use minimum to achieve target SpO2
Post-ROSC (return of spontaneous circulation) — titrate to SpO2 94–98%; avoid hyperoxia
Fire hazard — keep away from ignition sources; do not use in presence of oil or grease
Target SpO2 94–98% in most adults; 88–92% in COPD
sideEffects: Hypercapnic respiratory failure in susceptible COPD patients
Oxidative stress / free radical injury (very prolonged high FiO2)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Molecular oxygen (O2) is the terminal electron acceptor in the mitochondrial electron transport chain (oxidative phosphorylation), enabling ATP synthesis. Corrects hypoxaemia by increasing the alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient. High-flow oxygen is the treatment for carbon monoxide poisoning, displacing CO from haemoglobin.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: O2 is reduced to water (H2O) as the final step of the electron transport chain. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are produced as by-products and scavenged by antioxidants.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: CO2 (a metabolic by-product of aerobic metabolism) is exhaled. Excess O2 not taken up by tissues is exhaled unchanged.

adultDosing.maxDose: 100% FiO2 in cardiac arrest or CO poisoning
adultDosing.notes: Use nasal prongs 1–6L/min for mild hypoxia. Simple mask 5–10L/min. Non-rebreather mask 10–15L/min for severe hypoxia. Target SpO2 94–98% (88–92% in COPD).
Use 100% O2 during CPR. Post-ROSC: titrate to SpO2 94–98% and reduce FiO2 to minimum required.

adultDosing.frequency: Continuous until COHb <5% or symptom resolution
adultDosing.notes: High-flow 100% O2 is the primary treatment for CO poisoning. Reduces COHb half-life from 5–6 hours to ~60–90 minutes.

paediatricDosing.dose: Titrated to SpO2 94–98% (88–92% if chronic lung disease)
paediatricDosing.maxDose: 100% FiO2 in arrest or CO poisoning
paediatricDosing.notes: Use age-appropriate delivery device (nasal prongs, paediatric mask, BVM). Neonates/premature: titrate to SpO2 90–95% to avoid retinopathy risk.

name: Oxytocin
indications: Active management of third stage of labour (prevention of postpartum haemorrhage)
Induction and augmentation of labour (hospital setting, under obstetric direction)
contraindications: Before delivery of baby (do not give before baby is born in prehospital/FIFO setting)
Placenta praevia or vasa praevia (in context of induction)
precautions: Give ONLY after delivery of baby (and ideally after delivery of placenta in AMTSL)
IV bolus can cause profound hypotension and tachycardia — use slow IV push or diluted infusion
Avoid rapid IV bolus in cardiovascularly compromised patients
Water retention and hyponatraemia possible with prolonged infusion
Uterine hyperstimulation can cause foetal distress (in augmentation context)
Monitor BP, HR and uterine tone

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Endogenous posterior pituitary nonapeptide. Binds oxytocin G-protein-coupled receptors on uterine smooth muscle, activating phospholipase C → IP3/DAG → increased intracellular Ca2+ → myometrial contraction. Used post-delivery to stimulate uterine contraction and prevent/treat postpartum haemorrhage. Also causes vasodilation at high doses (risk of hypotension).
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Rapidly degraded by oxytocinase (cystine aminopeptidase) in plasma, liver, and kidneys. Not absorbed orally (protein degradation).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Metabolites excreted renally. Half-life 1–6 minutes (IV); very short duration.

adultDosing.indication: Active management of third stage of labour (AMTSL)
adultDosing.notes: Administer immediately after delivery of baby. IM route preferred in prehospital/remote setting. Do NOT give before delivery of baby.

adultDosing.frequency: Infuse at rate to control haemorrhage (typically 10–40 mU/min)
adultDosing.notes: Do NOT give as rapid undiluted IV bolus in PPH — risk of severe hypotension. Mix 10–40 IU in 500mL and titrate. If IV access not available: 10 IU IM.

paediatricDosing.notes: Oxytocin is not used in paediatric patients.

name: Packed Red Blood Cells (pRBC)
indications: Haemorrhagic shock unresponsive to crystalloid resuscitation
Symptomatic anaemia (Hb <70g/L in stable patients; higher threshold with cardiac disease or ongoing bleeding)
contraindications: Documented refusal of blood products (e.g., Jehovah's Witness — documented advance directive)
ABO/Rh incompatibility (transfusion reaction risk — must be group-matched or emergency O-negative)
precautions: Confirm ABO compatibility prior to transfusion when possible (use group-specific or O-negative in emergencies)
O-negative blood reserved for females of childbearing age or when group unknown (emergency use)
Transfusion reactions — monitor for fever, rigors, haemolysis, anaphylaxis, TRALI, TACO
Hypothermia risk — use blood warmer if available for large volumes
Hypocalcaemia — citrate in stored blood can chelate calcium; replace if large volume transfusion
Hyperkalaemia — stored pRBC has elevated potassium
Infection transmission risk (very low with modern testing)
Compatibility — transfuse through blood giving set (170–260 micron filter)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Provides erythrocytes (haemoglobin) to restore oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood in haemorrhagic or anaemic states. Each unit ~250–350 mL, raises haemoglobin by approximately 10 g/L in adults. Does not replace coagulation factors.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Transfused red blood cells integrate into the recipient's circulating pool. Haemoglobin is ultimately catabolised by the reticuloendothelial system to bilirubin, iron, and globin (same as endogenous RBCs).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Haemoglobin catabolites: iron recycled or stored as ferritin/haemosiderin; bilirubin conjugated in the liver and excreted in bile; globin peptides degraded to amino acids.

adultDosing.frequency: Transfuse until clinical haemostasis or Hb target achieved
adultDosing.notes: Administer via blood giving set with in-line filter. Target permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90mmHg) in uncontrolled haemorrhage until surgical haemostasis. In MTP: 1:1:1 ratio pRBC:FFP:platelets. Check group and screen when possible.

paediatricDosing.frequency: Transfuse over 2–4 hours (may be faster in active haemorrhage)
paediatricDosing.notes: Use O-negative blood if group unknown. Transfuse via paediatric blood giving set. Reassess after each bolus. Target Hb >70g/L (or higher in active bleeding).

name: Paracetamol
indications: Adjunct analgesia in multimodal pain management
contraindications: IV: known severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh Class C)
precautions: Hepatotoxicity risk with overdose — NAC antidote available
Hepatic impairment — reduce dose and frequency
Malnutrition, chronic alcoholism — increased hepatotoxicity risk (lower toxic threshold)
Concurrent use of other paracetamol-containing products — risk of inadvertent overdose
IV paracetamol — maximum rate per weight to avoid overdose
Renal impairment — increase dosing interval to every 6–8 hours
Avoid in patients with known G6PD deficiency (rare haemolytic risk)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Analgesic and antipyretic with uncertain mechanism. Likely acts centrally via inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis (COX inhibition in the CNS), activation of descending serotonergic pathways, and endocannabinoid system modulation. Has minimal peripheral anti-inflammatory activity (unlike NSAIDs).
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Predominantly hepatic (>95%): glucuronidation and sulphation to non-toxic conjugates. ~5% via CYP2E1/CYP3A4 to toxic reactive intermediate NAPQI, neutralised by glutathione. Overdose overwhelms glutathione stores → hepatocellular necrosis.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of conjugated metabolites. Half-life 1–4 hours; extended in hepatic disease.

adultDosing.notes: Standard tablet or dispersible. Do not exceed 4g/day from all sources.
Infuse over 15 minutes. Use 1g/100mL pre-prepared infusion. Reduce to 15mg/kg/dose in patients <50kg. Monitor for hypotension during infusion.

paediatricDosing.notes: Use paracetamol suspension (120mg/5mL or 250mg/5mL) for children. Measure by weight.
Infuse over 15 minutes. Dilute in NaCl 0.9% or 5% dextrose if needed. Monitor closely.

paediatricDosing.indication: Mild to moderate pain / fever (rectal — unable to take oral)
paediatricDosing.notes: Suppository available 125mg, 250mg, 500mg. Lower bioavailability than oral. Use when oral not possible.

name: Prednisolone
indications: Severe allergic reaction / anaphylaxis (adjunct after adrenaline)
Croup (moderate to severe — oral dexamethasone preferred)
Adrenal insufficiency (Addisonian crisis — IV hydrocortisone preferred acutely)
contraindications: Active untreated systemic infection (relative — balance with life-threatening indication)
precautions: Diabetes mellitus — hyperglycaemia
Peptic ulcer disease — GI bleeding risk (use with gastroprotective agent if prolonged)
Hypertension
Osteoporosis (prolonged use)
Immunosuppression (prolonged use)
Pregnancy — use only if clearly indicated
Psychiatric history — may precipitate mood disturbance
Short-course (3–5 days) prehospital use has minimal systemic side effects
Tuberculosis — may reactivate latent TB

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Synthetic glucocorticoid (active form of prednisone). Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription to reduce synthesis of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α), inhibit phospholipase A2, stabilise mast cells/eosinophils, and decrease vascular permeability. Used in asthma, COPD exacerbations, allergic reactions, and adrenal insufficiency.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism to inactive prednisolone sulphate and glucuronide conjugates. Prednisolone is already the active form (unlike prednisone which requires hepatic conversion).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of conjugates and unchanged drug. Half-life: plasma 2–3 hours; biological effect 12–36 hours.

adultDosing.notes: Onset 2–4 hours (oral). Take with food to reduce GI irritation. Do not stop abruptly if on long-term steroids.

adultDosing.frequency: Single dose stat or once daily for 3–5 days
adultDosing.notes: Not a substitute for adrenaline in anaphylaxis. Used as second-line to reduce late-phase reactions.

paediatricDosing.notes: Use oral solution (5mg/mL) or dispersible tablet for children. Onset 2–4 hours.
Not a substitute for adrenaline. Adjunct to reduce biphasic anaphylaxis risk.

name: Rocuronium Bromide
indications: Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) — neuromuscular blockade for intubation
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to rocuronium or bromides
No contraindications in emergency RSI when airway is immediately life-threatening
Relative: cannot intubate / cannot oxygenate scenario without surgical airway backup available
precautions: Must ONLY be administered by trained and credentialled paramedic/physician with RSI authorisation
Sugammadex must be available as reversal agent (16mg/kg for immediate reversal at RSI dose)
Complete respiratory and muscle paralysis — patient will be apnoeic; BVM ventilation and intubation must be immediately available
Cannot intubate / cannot ventilate — have surgical airway kit available
Prolonged effect in hepatic failure and Eaton-Lambert syndrome
Dose adjustment in myasthenia gravis
Hypothermia prolongs effect
Do not use without prior adequate induction of anaesthesia and analgesia (awareness risk)
sideEffects: Awareness under paralysis if inadequate induction

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Non-depolarising neuromuscular blocking agent (NMBA). Competitively antagonises acetylcholine at nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction, preventing depolarisation and causing flaccid paralysis. Does not cause fasciculations. Onset 60–90 seconds at intubating doses (1.2 mg/kg); used for RSI. Reversible by sugammadex.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism; primarily eliminated unchanged.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Primarily biliary/faecal (~50%) and renal (~30%) excretion unchanged. Half-life 1–2 hours; prolonged in hepatic impairment. Reversed by sugammadex (encapsulation).

adultDosing.notes: Administer as rapid IV bolus after induction agent (e.g., ketamine or propofol). Onset 45–60 seconds at 1.2mg/kg. Duration 30–60 minutes. Reversal: Sugammadex 16mg/kg IV immediately if cannot intubate.

paediatricDosing.notes: Same dose as adults by weight. Onset slightly faster in children. Have Sugammadex 16mg/kg available for reversal. Credentialled operator only.

name: Ropivacaine 0.75%
indications: Regional nerve blocks (fascia iliaca compartment block, femoral nerve block, intercostal block)
Local infiltration for prolonged post-procedural analgesia
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to amide local anaesthetics
Intravenous regional anaesthesia (Bier's block) — not recommended due to cardiac toxicity risk
Intrathecal use (not indicated for this concentration)
precautions: Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) — lipid emulsion 20% must be available
Aspirate before every injection to confirm extra-vascular placement
Maximum dose must not be exceeded — calculate total mg before administration
Hepatic impairment — reduced clearance
Cardiac disease — ropivacaine less cardiotoxic than bupivacaine but caution required
Pregnancy — use minimum effective dose
CNS toxicity signs (tinnitus, metallic taste, perioral numbness, seizures) indicate LAST
Advanced paramedic scope only — nerve block credentialling required
sideEffects: CNS toxicity — LAST (tinnitus, confusion, seizures at toxic levels)
Cardiovascular toxicity — arrhythmia, cardiac arrest (at very high doses)
Temporary numbness and motor block of affected region

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Long-acting amide local anaesthetic. Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in peripheral nerves, preventing action potential generation and propagation, producing reversible sensory and motor blockade. More cardioselective than bupivacaine, with lower risk of cardiac toxicity due to faster dissociation from sodium channels.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 to hydroxylated metabolites (primarily 3-hydroxyropivacaine).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites and glucuronide conjugates; <1% unchanged. Half-life 1.8 hours (nerve block); longer depending on site and dose.

adultDosing.indication: Fascia Iliaca Compartment Block (FICB) — hip/femur fracture
adultDosing.maxDose: 225mg (200mg for patients at risk — elderly, hepatic impairment)
adultDosing.notes: 0.75% = 7.5mg/mL. Use ultrasound guidance where available. Always aspirate before injecting. Onset 15–30 minutes. Duration 6–12 hours. Have lipid emulsion 20% available for LAST.
Aspirate before each injection. Use minimum volume needed. Duration 4–8 hours.

paediatricDosing.indication: Fascia Iliaca Compartment Block — femur/hip fracture (adolescents)
paediatricDosing.notes: Suitable for older children/adolescents (>20kg). Use appropriate concentration and volume. Credentialled operator only. Lipid emulsion 20% available.

name: Salbutamol Sulphate
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to salbutamol or adrenergic amines
precautions: Tachycardia — monitor HR; reduce dose if HR >130 and haemodynamically significant
Hypokalaemia — especially with high doses or in conjunction with other agents (theophylline, steroids, diuretics)
Hypoxaemia may worsen transiently due to V/Q mismatch — ensure oxygen supplementation
Diabetes — may cause hyperglycaemia
Cardiac arrhythmias — caution in underlying arrhythmia
Tremor common — dose-related
IV salbutamol — significant cardiovascular effects, use only under medical direction

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Short-acting selective β2-adrenoreceptor agonist. Activates adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → protein kinase A → phosphorylation of myosin light chain kinase → bronchial smooth muscle relaxation. At therapeutic doses, bronchodilates with minimal β1 cardiac effects. Also inhibits mast cell mediator release.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Inhaled: ~10% reaches lower airways, remainder swallowed and undergoes hepatic first-pass sulfation. IV: hepatic sulfation to inactive metabolites.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of metabolites (~70%) and unchanged drug (~30%). Half-life 3–8 hours.

adultDosing.frequency: Every 20 minutes (3 doses); then hourly as required
adultDosing.maxDose: Continuous nebulisation 5–15mg/hr in severe asthma under medical direction
adultDosing.notes: Dilute 5mg (1mL of 5mg/mL) in 2–3mL NaCl 0.9%. Drive with oxygen at 6–8L/min. In severe/life-threatening asthma, continuous nebulisation may be used.
4–8 puffs via spacer equivalent to nebulisation for mild–moderate asthma. Shake inhaler before each actuation.
IV salbutamol for severe/life-threatening asthma unresponsive to nebulised therapy. Under medical direction. Mix 5mg in 500mL (10mcg/mL). Continuous cardiac and SpO2 monitoring.

paediatricDosing.maxDose: Continuous nebulisation in severe asthma under direction
paediatricDosing.notes: Drive nebuliser with oxygen at 6–8L/min. In infants use 2.5mg. Monitor HR and SpO2.

paediatricDosing.maxDose: 4 puffs per dose (<6 years); 4–8 puffs per dose (>=6 years)
paediatricDosing.notes: Use spacer with face mask for children <5 years. 1 puff every 30–60 seconds.

name: Sodium Bicarbonate
indications: Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose — sodium channel poisoning, arrhythmias, hypotension
Severe metabolic acidosis with haemodynamic compromise
Cardiac arrest due to hyperkalaemia or TCA toxicity (specific indication)
precautions: Do not mix with calcium salts — precipitation
Do not mix with adrenaline — deactivation
Flush IV line well before and after administration
Excessive sodium and CO2 load — risk of hypernatraemia and worsening hypercapnia in respiratory failure
Check blood gases and electrolytes when available to guide dosing
In cardiac arrest, prioritise CPR quality, defibrillation and vasopressors — sodium bicarbonate is secondary
Extravasation causes tissue necrosis — ensure IV is patent
sideEffects: CO2 generation — may worsen ventilation if respiratory failure present

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Exogenous bicarbonate buffer. Reacts with hydrogen ions (H+) to form carbonic acid (H2CO3) → CO2 + H2O, transiently buffering acidaemia and raising serum pH and bicarbonate. Used in metabolic acidosis, tricyclic antidepressant toxicity (sodium loading stabilises myocardium), and hyperkalaemia.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Bicarbonate participates in the carbonic anhydrase buffer system. CO2 produced is exhaled by the lungs (adequate ventilation is essential).
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal regulation of bicarbonate reabsorption and excretion (proximal tubule). Excess bicarbonate excreted in urine if serum pH is alkaline.

adultDosing.indication: TCA overdose — arrhythmia / hypotension / wide QRS
adultDosing.frequency: May repeat every 3–5 minutes until QRS narrows or pH 7.45–7.55
adultDosing.maxDose: 3 mmol/kg initial bolus; infusion as directed
adultDosing.notes: 8.4% NaHCO3 = 1mmol/mL. Target blood pH 7.45–7.55. Monitor ECG continuously. Sodium loading and alkalosis narrow QRS and reduce arrhythmia risk in TCA toxicity.
Temporising measure only. Alkalosis shifts K+ intracellularly. Concurrent calcium gluconate (membrane stabilisation) and salbutamol (nebulised) also used.

adultDosing.indication: Cardiac arrest due to hyperkalaemia or TCA toxicity
adultDosing.notes: Given during CPR when hyperkalaemia or TCA toxicity strongly suspected. Flush line before and after. Do not co-administer with calcium.

paediatricDosing.indication: Cardiac arrest / severe metabolic acidosis (specific indications)
paediatricDosing.notes: Dilute 8.4% to 4.2% (1:1 with water for injection) in neonates and infants to reduce hyperosmolality risk. Reserve for specific indications (hyperkalaemia, TCA, prolonged arrest with severe acidosis).

name: Sodium Fluorescein (Ophthalmic)
indications: Detection of corneal epithelial defects (abrasions, ulcers, foreign body tracks)
Assessment of corneal integrity following trauma or chemical exposure
contraindications: Suspected penetrating eye injury — do not apply topically (risk of dye entering anterior chamber)
Soft contact lenses in situ — must be removed first
precautions: Remove soft contact lenses prior to use — fluorescein permanently stains soft lenses
Use single-use minims or individual strips to avoid cross-contamination
Discard any remaining solution after use
Cobalt blue light source required to interpret staining — examination in standard white light is unreliable
Topical anaesthetic may be required in painful eyes to facilitate examination — requires on-call doctor authorisation
sideEffects: Temporary yellow-orange discolouration of skin and tears (resolves rapidly)
Hypersensitivity reaction — very rare with topical use

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Sodium fluorescein is a water-soluble fluorescent dye. Intact corneal epithelium has tight junctions that exclude the dye. When epithelial integrity is disrupted, fluorescein penetrates the stroma and fluoresces bright green under cobalt blue (450–490 nm) illumination, making defects readily visible.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Minimal systemic absorption with topical ophthalmic application. Any absorbed dye is taken up by hepatocytes and excreted into bile.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Primarily excreted via the biliary/faecal route; small amount excreted renally. Causes transient yellow discolouration of urine and skin.

adultDosing.dose: 1 drop of 2% solution into lower conjunctival fornix
adultDosing.maxDose: Single application; may repeat if required for re-examination
adultDosing.notes: Instil into lower conjunctival fornix. Ask patient to blink to distribute. Examine immediately under cobalt blue light. Irrigate excess dye after examination.

adultDosing.dose: 1 fluorescein-impregnated strip moistened with 1 drop sterile saline
adultDosing.maxDose: Single application; may repeat if required
adultDosing.notes: Touch moistened strip tip to lower conjunctival fornix. Ask patient to blink. Examine under cobalt blue light.

paediatricDosing.dose: 1 drop of 2% solution into lower conjunctival fornix
paediatricDosing.notes: Same as adult dosing. Instil carefully with child supine if required. Parental assistance may be needed to keep the eye open.

name: Sumatriptan
indications: Acute migraine with or without aura (first-line if triptans not contraindicated)
Cluster headache (SC injection is most effective route)
Refractory migraine where simple analgesia has failed
contraindications: Known coronary artery disease, Prinzmetal's angina, or history of MI
Concurrent or recent (within 24 hours) use of other triptans or ergotamine
Concurrent use of MAOIs (or within 14 days)
precautions: Do not use to treat headache not clearly consistent with migraine — do not give if diagnosis uncertain
Not for prophylaxis — acute treatment only
First dose should ideally be taken under medical supervision in patients with cardiac risk factors
Can be given at any stage of migraine attack (not just at onset)
If no response to first dose, do not give second dose for same attack
Patients with migraine 15+ days/month should be referred for prophylaxis
sideEffects: Chest tightness or pressure (common — usually not cardiac)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Selective agonist at 5-HT1B (vasoconstriction of cranial blood vessels) and 5-HT1D (inhibition of trigeminal nociceptive transmission) receptors. Causes intracranial vasoconstriction and inhibits release of neuropeptides causing neurogenic inflammation.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic (MAO-A). Oral bioavailability ~14% (significant first-pass effect). SC bioavailability ~97%.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion of inactive metabolites. Half-life ~2 hours.

adultDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat once after 2 hours if partial response
adultDosing.notes: Start with 50 mg — increase to 100 mg if 50 mg inadequate in future attacks. May repeat after 2 hours if headache recurs or partial response. If no response to first dose, do not give second dose for same attack.

adultDosing.indication: Acute migraine or cluster headache — subcutaneous (rapid onset)
adultDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat once after ≥1 hour if partial response
adultDosing.notes: SC route gives fastest onset (~10–20 minutes) — preferred for severe migraine or cluster headache. Inject into lateral thigh or upper arm. Auto-injector available.

adultDosing.frequency: Single dose; may repeat once after 2 hours
adultDosing.notes: Onset ~15–30 minutes. Useful when nausea limits oral route.

name: Tenecteplase (TNKase)
indications: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) when primary PCI is unavailable within 120 minutes of first medical contact and symptom onset is <6 hours (or up to 12 hours with ongoing ischaemia)
Pharmaco-invasive strategy: pre-hospital fibrinolysis followed by planned PCI — standard at remote mine sites with long transport times
contraindications: ABSOLUTE: Previous stroke or TIA within 6 months
ABSOLUTE: Haemorrhagic stroke at any time
ABSOLUTE: Intracranial neoplasm, AVM, or aneurysm
ABSOLUTE: Recent (within 3 months) significant closed head or facial trauma
RELATIVE: Severe uncontrolled hypertension (SBP >180 or DBP >110 mmHg) on presentation
RELATIVE: Major surgery or significant trauma within 3 months
RELATIVE: CPR >10 minutes with traumatic resuscitation
RELATIVE: Recent (2–4 weeks) internal bleeding or biopsy of non-compressible site
RELATIVE: Current anticoagulant therapy (INR >1.7 or therapeutic heparin)
RELATIVE: Pregnancy or within 1 week postpartum
precautions: Only administer with clear ECG-confirmed STEMI (≥2mm ST elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads, or LBBB)
Confirm aspirin 300 mg has been given
Avoid non-compressible arterial/venous punctures (femoral) after administration
Monitor closely for bleeding, hypotension, reperfusion arrhythmias
Reperfusion arrhythmias (AIVR) are expected and benign — do not treat unless haemodynamically compromising
Give heparin or enoxaparin as anticoagulant adjunct (hospital-initiated or per local protocol)
15% of patients experience failure of fibrinolysis — arrange transfer to cath lab regardless
Weight-based dosing is critical — prepare and double-check before administration
Medical officer authorisation should be obtained before administration in the pre-hospital setting

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Modified tPA — binds to fibrin in thrombus and activates plasminogen, converting it to plasmin which degrades fibrin clot. More fibrin-specific than streptokinase, with longer half-life allowing single bolus administration.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism. Onset of fibrinolysis within minutes of administration.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Hepatic. Half-life ~20–24 minutes (initial); terminal half-life ~90–130 minutes.

adultDosing.notes:
WEIGHT-BASED DOSING — administer as single IV bolus over 5–10 seconds:
• <60 kg: 30 mg (6 mL)
• 60–69 kg: 35 mg (7 mL)
• 70–79 kg: 40 mg (8 mL)
• 80–89 kg: 45 mg (9 mL)
• ≥90 kg: 50 mg (10 mL)

Reconstitute with 10 mL sterile water for injection. Administer via dedicated IV line. DO NOT shake — gently mix by swirling. Avoid IM injections after administration. Ensure aspirin 300 mg given before or concurrent with tenecteplase.

name: Tranexamic Acid
indications: Major trauma with haemorrhage — within 3 hours of injury
Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) — within 3 hours of delivery
Major haemorrhage from any cause (surgical, GI, trauma)
contraindications: Active thromboembolic disease (DVT, PE, stroke, MI — relative contraindication; balance with haemorrhage severity)
Seizure disorder — TXA can be convulsant at high doses (IV undiluted, rapid infusion)
precautions: Time-critical — administer within 3 hours of injury/haemorrhage onset for maximum benefit (CRASH-2 trial evidence)
Benefit greatly diminishes after 3 hours; may be harmful if given >3 hours post-injury
Administer IV slowly — rapid infusion may cause hypotension and seizures
Renal impairment — dose reduction required (renally excreted)
Concurrent pro-coagulant therapy — thrombosis risk
Colour change or precipitate — do not use if discolouration present
Evidence of benefit in trauma and PPH; not routine in cardiac arrest without haemorrhage
sideEffects: Seizures (high-dose rapid IV — rare at recommended doses)
Thromboembolic events (theoretical — not demonstrated at recommended doses)
Visual disturbances (colour vision — rare, prolonged use)

mechanismOfAction.mechanism: Synthetic lysine analogue. Competitively inhibits plasminogen activation by blocking plasminogen's lysine-binding sites, preventing fibrin degradation (antifibrinolytic). Preserves existing clot integrity. Does not directly promote clot formation. Evidence: CRASH-2 trial showed reduced haemorrhagic mortality when given within 3 hours of injury.
mechanismOfAction.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism; excreted predominantly unchanged.
mechanismOfAction.excretion: Renal excretion ~95% unchanged. Half-life 2–3 hours; dose reduction required in renal impairment.

adultDosing.frequency: Single loading dose; may be followed by 1g infusion over 8 hours in hospital
adultDosing.notes: Must be given within 3 hours of injury. Dilute 1g in 100mL NaCl 0.9% or 5% dextrose. Infuse over 10 minutes. For prehospital use, 1g IV over 10 minutes is sufficient.

adultDosing.frequency: May repeat 1g if bleeding continues after 30 minutes or restarts within 24 hours
adultDosing.notes: Give within 3 hours of delivery. Administer concurrent with oxytocin and uterine massage. Dilute in 100mL NaCl 0.9%. Give slowly — do not push rapidly.

paediatricDosing.notes: Administer within 3 hours of injury. Dilute in appropriate volume NaCl 0.9%. Evidence in paediatric trauma is extrapolated from adult data (CRASH-2); use under medical direction.
